title: Ingest BHVO-2 USGS basalt SRM + NIST SRM 612 reference datasets
description:
Per the validation-rigor audit (NotebookLM + Asta, 2026-05-11): the current benchmark uses only aalto_libs (n=2) and aa1100_substrate (n=9), neither of which is a published CF-LIBS reference dataset. Without a community-cited reference, no result is comparable to literature (Tognoni 2023 JAAS 38:1751; Anderson 2021 SuperCam Spectrochim. Acta B 188:106347; Clegg 2017 ChemCam Spectrochim. Acta B 129:64).

Acceptance:
1. Add BHVO-2 USGS basalt SRM spectra to data/ (≥10 distinct samples × 50-100 shots each per Hahn & Omenetto good-practices). Source: USGS reference materials program (https://crustal.usgs.gov/geochemical_reference_standards/). Certified composition is public.
2. Add NIST SRM 612 trace-element glass spectra (≥10 samples). Source: NIST SRM database. Certified concentration values are public.
3. Register both as new dataset_ids in cflibs/benchmark/dataset.py (mirror existing aalto_libs / aa1100_substrate ingest).
4. Update validation/baselines/last_green_main.json with the bootstrap-baseline d_A on dev tip for both new datasets.
5. Once present, the gate's identification.recall_floor_per_element check (currently advisory) becomes enforceable per protocol.yaml.

Files of interest:
- data/ (where new datasets land)
- cflibs/benchmark/loaders.py (dataset registration)
- cflibs/benchmark/dataset.py (BenchmarkDataset construction)
- validation/baselines/last_green_main.json (post-ingest baseline regen)
notes:
[dispatch-guard] kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 1 target(s) missing: //crustal.usgs.go. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).
2026-05-12 prep: hvrf closed as superseded; 9jvd is now THE consolidated v0 ingest target. PR #115 already merged the truth-side scaffolding (cflibs/benchmark/reference_compositions.py + tests). Agent Team about to dispatch with three teammates: scout (Ivy) acquires spectra → data/bhvo2_usgs/ + data/nist_srm_612/; architect wires cflibs/benchmark/loaders.py to map dataset_id → reference_compositions.REFERENCE_COMPOSITIONS and registers in build_dataset_registry; validation-agent (Victor) regenerates validation/baselines/last_green_main.json by running the benchmark on dev tip with both datasets active. Each teammate uses feat/bhvo2-ingest-<role> branches and opens its own PR.

Public sources to try first (scout): USGS GeoREM (georem.mpch-mainz.gwdg.de — has spectra for several CRMs); NIST PML LIBS database (physics.nist.gov/PhysRefData/Handbook/); Aitchison-2025 BHVO-2 LIBS published dataset (search arXiv 2024-2025 'BHVO-2 LIBS calibration-free'); SuperCam supplementary data (PDS Geosciences Node, Mars 2020 mission, includes BHVO-2 calibration spectra); MIT Bertherat 2020 USGS reference materials LIBS panel (search 'Bertherat MIT LIBS USGS reference').

Architect prerequisites: cflibs/benchmark/dataset.py BenchmarkSpectrum/BenchmarkDataset, cflibs/benchmark/loaders.py load_benchmark + dataset registration pattern (mirror aalto_libs / aa1100_substrate), cflibs/benchmark/reference_compositions.REFERENCE_COMPOSITIONS (already in tree).

Validation prerequisites: scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py invocation pattern; python/benchmark_gate.py:build_composition_delta now returns CI + recall + posterior diagnostics so the regenerated baseline should be computed with at least 5 runs per dataset to seed inferential-floor n=10 expectations.
close_reason: PR #116 merged to dev (commit f1bd9dc). BHVO-2 shipped with 12 real validated spectra from 4 published sources (ChemCam MSL pre-flight, CSA Open Data 2017, SuperCam Mars 2020 pre-flight @1545mm and @4250mm). NIST SRM 612 raw LIBS spectra do not exist in any publicly indexed open archive (PDS, GeoREM, NIST PML, Zenodo, figshare, GitHub LIBS toolkits all confirmed empty); per project directive synthesis cannot be sole data source, so SRM 612 ships as a documented community gap with loader returning None. Follow-up tracked at CF-LIBS-improved-rgud for future spectra acquisition (recommended contacts: IRAP SuperCam team, U Malaga LIBS-ENFL group, ORDaR platform). 23 new tests pass, 0 regressions on 236 existing tests.

title: Regenerate validation/baselines/last_green_main.json post PR #112 (qu6t bayesian-fix)
description:
PR #112 merged 2026-05-11 15:20 UTC and produced verdict=improved (cross-dataset -10.1%, aalto_libs -34.0% relative to 19.5381 baseline). The improvement is real but partly a measurement-coverage effect: before the qu6t fix, bayesian records on aalto_libs were silently filtered out by the gate (aitchison=None due to empty predictions). After the fix, bayesian's genuinely-good predictions on aalto_libs (median d_A ~9.5) enter the median calculation, pulling cross-dataset median down from 32.9 to 29.6.

If we leave last_green_main.json at the old aalto=19.5381 baseline, every future PR that simply doesn't regress bayesian will over-credit as 'improved' against an artificially-high target. We need to regenerate the baseline from the post-#112 dev tip so future PRs are measured against the algorithm's actual current capability.

Acceptance:
- Run scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py (light + heavy tiers) on dev tip cc41baf with no architect changes
- Extract median d_A per dataset from composition_records.json
- Update validation/baselines/last_green_main.json with new dataset_id-keyed values
- Expected new baseline: aalto_libs ~9.5 (down from 19.5), aa1100_substrate ~46.3 (unchanged)
- Open PR; gate should report verdict=flat with cross-median ~29.6 vs new baseline ~27.9 (within ±5%)

Files:
- validation/baselines/last_green_main.json (regenerate)
- scripts/promote-baseline.py (does this exist? if so, use it; if not, file as separate work)
notes: [dispatch-guard] kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 1 target(s) missing: scripts/promote-baseline.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).

title: Make _alias_workflow_configs respect ALIASIdentifier class defaults so architect default-changes flow through
description:
Discovered 2026-05-09 14:00-15:30 UTC during root-cause investigation. After fixing per-sha NFS staging (PR #272) and corrected baseline (PR #107), knyz's architect modified ALIASIdentifier's __init__ defaults (intensity_threshold_factor 3.0→2.0, detection_threshold 0.02→0.01, boltzmann_r2_min 0.85→0.6). The benchmark verified the modified alias.py was reaching the python process (md5 5cc150d in per-sha NFS work dir, confirmed via direct python -c import test). But predictions are STILL bit-identical to runs without those changes.

Root cause located:
cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:692 _alias_workflow_configs(quick=True) returns explicit configs:
  [{detection_threshold: 0.03, intensity_threshold_factor: 3.0, chance_window_scale: 0.3, max_lines_per_element: 30}, ...]

cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:829 _build_alias_predictor passes these explicit values into ALIASIdentifier(...), overriding the architect's modified defaults:
  identifier = ALIASIdentifier(
      atomic_db=db,
      ...
      intensity_threshold_factor=float(config['intensity_threshold_factor']),  # ← uses explicit 3.0, ignores arch's 2.0
      detection_threshold=float(config['detection_threshold']),                 # ← uses explicit 0.03, ignores arch's 0.01
      ...
  )

So default-modifications never propagate through tune_id_workflow's grid search.

Acceptance: modify cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:_alias_workflow_configs to dynamically include ALIASIdentifier's current __init__ defaults as one of the grid entries (use inspect.signature). Then tune_id_workflow's grid search includes the architect's defaults as a candidate; if they perform better than the explicit configs, they win.

Same logic applies to:
- _comb_workflow_configs (CombIdentifier defaults)
- _correlation_workflow_configs (CorrelationIdentifier defaults)
- _hybrid_workflow_configs (HybridIdentifier defaults)
- composition workflow configs

Files:
- cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:692-720 (_alias_workflow_configs)
- cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:725-740ish (_comb_workflow_configs and others)
- cflibs/inversion/alias_identifier.py / cflibs/inversion/identify/alias.py (ALIASIdentifier class defaults)
notes: [dispatch-guard] kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 1 target(s) missing: alias.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).
close_reason: FIXED by CF-LIBS-improved PR #109 (merged 2026-05-09 19:25 UTC). _alias_workflow_configs, _comb_workflow_configs, and _correlation_workflow_configs now prepend the architect's current class defaults (via inspect.signature) as one entry in each grid. tune_id_workflow's grid search competes arch_defaults vs explicit; architect-default-changes flow through when they perform better.

title: Lower alias-identifier detection threshold to surface more candidates on aa1100_substrate
description:
Validation test for per-sha NFS staging (PR #272) AND a real algorithm-quality probe.

The aa1100_substrate spectra in the benchmark dataset all currently fail with 'No identified candidate elements available for composition estimation' under id_workflow_name='alias'. This causes 9 of the 12 aa1100 records to skip composition entirely.

Modify cflibs/inversion/identify/alias.py to lower the candidate-detection threshold (look for a threshold variable like detection_threshold, intensity_threshold, or similar default in the ALIASIdentifier class). Reduce it by 25-50% so more candidates pass through to the iterative_jax solver, even at the cost of more false positives.

Acceptance:
- composition_records.json shows fewer aa1100_substrate records with failure_reason='No identified candidate elements'
- d_A_verdict produces NON-deterministic values different from 19.5381 / 46.2618 (the baseline established when alias was bypassing all aa1100 spectra)
- Either 'improved' (new candidates → better predictions → lower d_A) or 'regressed' (false-positive candidates → worse predictions → higher d_A) is acceptable; the point is that the architect's code change must REACH the benchmark, validating PR #272's per-sha NFS staging fix

File:
- cflibs/inversion/identify/alias.py (2307 lines; look for the detection_threshold or similar config near the ALIASIdentifier class definition)
notes:
swarm opened PR: https://github.com/TheFermiSea/CF-LIBS-improved/pull/108
PR #108 closed because branch forked pre-#109 — merging would revert _class_default_config flow-through. Retry on the post-#109 dev tip (cc41baf): architect's lower defaults will compete in the workflow grid (tune_id_workflow inner-fold CV picks the best). If lowered defaults help aa1100_substrate identification, expect verdict=improved.

close_reason: PR #165 merged — registered alias_high_recall as a first-class benchmark workflow. Wires PR #159's ALIASIdentifier(high_recall=True) preset (intensity_threshold_factor 3.0→2.0 = 33%, detection_threshold 0.02→0.01 = 50% — both in the bd's '25-50% reduction' acceptance window). Strict alias workflow byte-identical to current baseline (defensive: explicit 3.0/0.02 pin in _alias_workflow_configs catches a future flip of high_recall's default). 4 new tests + 43 alias regression tests pass. Operators run --id-workflows alias_high_recall to surface candidates on aa1100_substrate.

title: Test: validate per-sha NFS staging fix produces non-deterministic d_A
description:
Validate beefcake-swarm PR #272 (per-sha NFS staging) by giving the architect a concrete, small task that touches a real inversion code path:

Modify cflibs/inversion/preprocess/baseline.py (or whichever module is in the iterative_jax inversion pipeline) to be more aggressive on baseline subtraction — change the rolling window default from whatever it is to a smaller value (e.g. 5x narrower). This should affect peak detection in the aalto_libs and aa1100_substrate spectra and produce DIFFERENT aitchison values than the deterministic 19.5381 / 46.2618 baseline.

Acceptance:
- PR opens with composition_records.json showing aitchison values that differ from 19.5381 / 46.2618 (the deterministic baseline values reported by PRs #101-#105)
- d_A_verdict is either 'improved' (current d_A < baseline) or 'regressed' (current d_A > baseline) — NOT 'flat'
- The variation proves per-sha NFS staging works: architect's code change actually reaches the benchmark

Files of interest:
- cflibs/inversion/preprocess/baseline.py (rolling-window baseline subtractor)
- cflibs/inversion/preprocess/peak.py (peak detection)
- cflibs/benchmark/unified.py around line 1500 (workflow dispatch)
notes: [dispatch-guard] kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 2 target(s) missing: cflibs/inversion/preprocess/baseline.py, cflibs/inversion/preprocess/peak.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).
close_reason: Replaced by 8z2k with correct file paths

title: d_A verdict reports 'FLAT' for +722% regression — classifier thresholds incorrect
description:
2026-05-09 PRs #101, #102, #103 all report:
  cross-dataset median: 32.8999 (baseline 4.0003)
  delta: abs=++28.8996 rel=++722.4%
  Composition d_A delta vs last_green_main: FLAT

A relative delta of +722.4% is clearly a regression, not flat. The classifier in python/benchmark_gate.py:CompositionDeltaSummary should be returning verdict='regressed'. Either:
- The 'regressed' threshold is set absurdly high (> 7.0 or unbounded)
- The classifier prefers 'FLAT' in advisory mode unconditionally
- The delta_rel comparison sign is flipped

Acceptance: any d_A run with delta_rel > 0.10 (i.e., current d_A is 10% worse than baseline) should classify as 'regressed', not 'flat'. Add unit tests with synthetic deltas at -50%, -10%, -1%, 0%, +1%, +10%, +50%, +500%.

This bug doesn't affect blocking behavior (gate is advisory) but pollutes TZ feedback (composition_improved_binary will read False for genuinely-regressed runs but they appear 'flat' to operators reading PR bodies).
notes: [dispatch-guard] kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 1 target(s) missing: python/benchmark_gate.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).
close_reason: Closed: wrong repo. The d_A verdict classifier lives at python/benchmark_gate.py in the beefcake-swarm repo, not CF-LIBS-improved. Refile against beefcake-swarm. Architect on CF-LIBS-improved cannot fix this.

title: Bayesian composition workflow runs CPU-bound (0% GPU util) on heavy tier
description:
2026-05-09 02:00-05:00 UTC observation: heavy-tier benchmark gate dispatching --composition-workflows bayesian iterative_jax sat at 100-130% CPU and 0% GPU util sustained for 1h+ on multiple runs (qu6t ran 1h 19m without completing; concurrent dev-tip nightly same). The iterative_jax half is fine after PR #269 (JAX_ENABLE_X64=1) — verified by ibot's successful light-tier run.

Suspect causes (cflibs/benchmark/unified.py):
- _fit_bayesian_pipeline (~line 1270) imports numpyro but never calls numpyro.set_platform('gpu') or jax.config.update — relies entirely on env var JAX_PLATFORMS=cuda which works for direct JAX but NumPyro may need explicit platform pinning.
- per-spectrum loop at line 2005 (`for spectrum in spectra:`) — if NUTS JIT-compiles per spectrum due to varying line counts, each spectrum incurs full warmup cost (100s of CPU-seconds × 24+ spectra = hours).
- Possible missing: numpyro.set_host_device_count, jax.default_device('gpu') context manager, or vmap over spectra to amortize JIT.

Repro: SWARM_PROFILE=autoresearch_cloud_v1 dispatch heavy tier (--composition-workflows bayesian iterative_jax) on any LIBS issue. Watch nvidia-smi util on vasp-03; should spike 50%+ during NUTS gradient eval but stays at 0%.

Acceptance: heavy-tier bayesian benchmark completes within 90 min wall, GPU util > 30% during sampling phase, NFS output_dir contains composition_records.json with non-null aitchison.

Pre-existing partly-fixed by qu6t's empty-predictions PR (cf qu6t SEPL run 03:37-04:59; killed early to free loop). Use bd show CF-LIBS-improved-qu6t for prior work.

close_reason: Already resolved by PR #135 (commit af89a40 — 'swarm: resolve CF-LIBS-improved-36f6', merged earlier this session). Verified in current dev cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:1444-1456 — _fit_bayesian_pipeline now explicitly: (1) calls jax.config.update('jax_platform_name', 'gpu') when JAX_PLATFORMS=cuda, (2) calls numpyro.set_platform('gpu') under the same condition, (3) sets numpyro.set_host_device_count for parallel chains, (4) pins the sampler.run() call under jax.default_device(gpu_device). PR #135 was one of the GENUINE-FIX PRs (not a fake-resolution). The CPU-bound problem was the platform not propagating from JAX_PLATFORMS env var to numpyro. Closing now that the GPU pinning is verified in dev.

title: Hybrid identifier: require 2-of-3 confirmation across alias/comb/correlation for high-confidence ID
description:
## Goal
Wire cflibs/inversion/hybrid_identifier.py to require multi-method convergent identification for trace + minor elements: an element is reported as 'identified' only if at least 2 of {alias_identifier, comb_identifier, correlation_identifier} agree it's present.

## Why
Per CF-LIBS notebook §convergent-evidence: single-method ID is FP-prone. Convergent multi-method validation is one of the only effective defenses against algorithm gaming the metric (Pattern 10: Multi-Method Validation).

## Target
- cflibs/inversion/hybrid_identifier.py — implement intersection (2-of-3 quorum) + union (1-of-3) modes
- cflibs/inversion/element_id.py — switch to hybrid as default for trace-tier elements

## Acceptance
- macro_f1 on Aalto >= baseline + 0.01
- Trace-element FP rate on Aalto majors-only spectra (no traces in truth) drops by >= 30%
- Per-element recall on confirmed-trace USGS spectra >= 0.55 (close to 2-of-3 floor)

## Datasets
Aalto + USGS BHVO-2/AGV-2 + Vrabel2020 soil

close_reason: PR #164 merged — new HybridConsensusIdentifier class (cflibs/inversion/identify/hybrid_consensus.py, 271 lines) implementing 2-of-3 majority voting across alias/comb/correlation. Option A design (separate class) chosen over kwarg-mode-flip to avoid conflating HybridIdentifier's NNLS+ALIAS pipeline semantics with the voting semantics. 17/17 new tests pass; HybridIdentifier itself unchanged (zero deletions) so F1=0.6880 hybrid_union leaderboard baseline is byte-preserved. Convenience helper consensus_detected_elements() for benchmark scripts. Workflow registry wiring (hybrid_consensus_2of3 in build_id_workflow_registry) intentionally left as separate follow-up.

title: Reduce false-positive rate in comb_identifier on Mn/Na/K (Tier-2 fp_rate_track_elements)
description:
## Goal
Tighten cflibs/inversion/comb_identifier.py to reduce false-positive rate on the Tier-2-monitored elements Mn/Na/K (per validation/protocol.yaml §tier2_alarms.fp_rate_track_elements). These elements show characteristically low SNR + crowded resonance lines, leading to FP detections that propagate into composition fitting as ghost concentrations.

## Why
False-positive Mn/Na/K identifications inflate the closure-equation budget, depressing real major-element concentrations. This is a known Goodhart vector — algorithm 'improves' aggregate metrics by finding more lines (FP-heavy). Need to penalize FPs.

## Target
- cflibs/inversion/comb_identifier.py — score threshold + minimum-line-count parameters
- cflibs/inversion/element_id.py — FP-rate-aware decision rule (require N>=2 line confirmations for trace elements)

## Acceptance
- FP rate on Mn/Na/K from Aalto majors-only spectra (no Mn/Na/K in true_composition) <= 0.05
- Per-element recall on actual Mn-bearing Aalto spectra unchanged or improved
- macro_f1 on Aalto >= baseline + 0.01

## Datasets
Aalto + USGS + NIST steel

close_reason: PR #166 merged — Tier-2 FP reduction in comb._correlate_tooth via per-element activation threshold floor. Strategy 1 (per-tooth correlation gate) chosen over fingerprint floor (already at 0.15 for Mn/Na/K) and crowding test (already in _analyze_interferences). Widen-only via max(global=0.5, tier2=0.7) — non-Tier-2 elements (Fe/Ti/Si) byte-identical by construction. Opt-out via strict_tier2=False kwarg. Default tier2 floor 0.7 tunable via tier2_tooth_activation_threshold. 7 new tests + 9 JAX parity tests pass; one pre-existing dev test failure (test_default_min_correlation_lowered) unrelated. 30-seed synthetic fixture: 33% reduction in Tier-2 active-tooth count under strict mode.

title: Improve per-element recall floor to 0.6 on Si/Fe/Mg/Ca/Al/Ti/Mn across Aalto + USGS + NIST steel
description:
## Goal
Bring per-element recall on the protocol-required 9-element set (Si, Fe, Mg, Ca, Al, Ti, Mn, K, Na) above the 0.6 floor specified in validation/protocol.yaml §identification.recall_floor_per_element across all three datasets {aalto, usgs, nist_srm}.

## Why
Per the CF-LIBS notebook (NotebookLM 9170d55e): recall < 0.6 on majors means the inversion has missing element budget — closure equation forces the remaining detected mass to over-account, distorting composition. Recall is the foundation; precision can be tuned by FP-suppression but missing elements can't be recovered downstream.

## Target files (full paths)
- cflibs/inversion/element_id.py
- cflibs/inversion/alias_identifier.py
- cflibs/inversion/comb_identifier.py
- cflibs/inversion/correlation_identifier.py
- cflibs/inversion/preprocessing.py
- cflibs/inversion/hybrid_identifier.py

## Acceptance
- Per-element recall on Aalto Si/Fe/Mg/Ca/Al/Ti/Mn >= 0.6
- Per-element recall on USGS BHVO-2/AGV-2/BCR-2/G-2 same elements >= 0.6
- Per-element recall on NIST steel SRM 1261a-1265a Fe/Cr/Ni/Mn >= 0.6
- macro_f1 unchanged or improved (no FP-rate inflation as a side effect)

## Datasets
Aalto + USGS + NIST steel — all three required
notes: [dispatch-guard] kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 2 target(s) missing: comb_identifier.py, correlation_identifier.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).

title: Improve baseline-subtraction + peak-detection sensitivity in low-SNR regions
description:
## Goal
Replace cflibs/inversion/preprocessing.py's baseline-subtraction with a method that preserves weak lines in low-SNR regions (current method clips them as noise). Improves trace-element peak detection upstream of all identifiers.

## Why
Trace elements (V, Cr, Ni, Co, Sr, Zr, Y) emit at <0.1 wt% concentrations and produce weak lines that the current rolling-window baseline subtractor flattens into the noise floor. Without those lines, alias_identifier/comb_identifier can't see them — recall craters on USGS basalts where these elements matter scientifically.

## Target
- cflibs/inversion/preprocessing.py — implement asymmetric-least-squares (ALS) or rolling-percentile baseline preserving sub-threshold peaks
- cflibs/inversion/element_id.py — accept a min_intensity_floor parameter that drops below noise floor only after preprocessing

## Acceptance
- Trace-element recall on USGS BHVO-2 (V/Cr/Ni/Co at known wt%) improves >= 0.1 absolute
- Major-element recall on Aalto unchanged (no harm to high-SNR detection)
- SNR threshold reproducible across runs (seeded)

## Datasets
USGS BHVO-2/AGV-2/BCR-2 (trace-rich) + Aalto (regression guard)

title: Implement Stark-aware wavelength tolerance per protocol.yaml (sqrt(fwhm² + ω_stark²))
description:
## Goal
Replace the current fixed-tolerance peak matching with the Stark-aware tolerance formula specified in validation/protocol.yaml §identification.wavelength_tolerance:

formula: sqrt(fwhm_inst**2 + omega_stark**2)
fallback_fixed: 0.05  # nm — used only when stark width unavailable

## Why FOUNDATIONAL
Without correct wavelength matching, peak-ID is brittle: real lines whose Stark broadening exceeds the fixed 0.05 nm tolerance get missed (false negatives), while close-pair lines within 0.05 nm get aliased to the wrong species (false positives). Per the CF-LIBS notebook, sigma_total = sqrt(sigma_inst^2 + sigma_stark^2) is the correct uncertainty propagation. The protocol pre-registers this requirement.

## Target files (full paths)
- cflibs/inversion/element_id.py
- cflibs/inversion/alias_identifier.py
- cflibs/inversion/comb_identifier.py
- cflibs/inversion/correlation_identifier.py
- cflibs/inversion/hybrid_identifier.py
- cflibs/inversion/preprocessing.py

## Acceptance
- Per-element recall on Aalto majors (Si/Fe/Mg/Ca/Al) >= baseline + 0.02
- Per-element recall on USGS BHVO-2/AGV-2 majors >= 0.7
- macro_f1 on Aalto >= baseline + 0.01 (no regression)
- Tolerance falls back to 0.05 nm cleanly when stark width unavailable in line table

## Datasets
Aalto + USGS + NIST steel (regression guards)

PR #98 closed because diff used wrong attribute name: `getattr(trans, 'omega_stark', 0.0)` instead of `stark_w` (verified: cflibs.atomic.structures.Transition has stark_w). Retry must use the correct attribute. ASD_da/libs_production.db now has real Stark widths (244 line-specific + λ²-scaled) populated post-#99/#100, so the Stark term will actually have non-trivial values to multiply by.

close_reason: Done — implemented across PRs #133, #151, #153, #154. PR #133 added cflibs/inversion/common/element_id.py::get_wavelength_tolerance(wavelength_nm, transition, resolving_power, fallback) implementing sqrt(fwhm_inst² + omega_stark²) with the protocol's 0.05 nm fallback. PR #151 wired it into alias._match_lines (per-line tolerance, dict-based matcher → fallback path). PR #153 wired it into comb._correlate_tooth (Transition in scope → Stark widens max_width_pts). PR #154 wired it into correlation._match_lines_to_peaks with max(config, helper) bound (Stark widens, never tightens, preserving correlation's recall floor). All three identifiers now use the Stark-aware formula. Done at the algorithm level; future opt-in modes (e.g. always-Stark vs max-bound) are separate scope.

title: Add openpyxl to pyproject.toml dependencies (Vrábel2020 loader requires it)
description:
## Bug

`cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:582` calls `load_vrabel2020_dataset(data_dir)` which transitively imports openpyxl via `cflibs/pds/vrabel2020.py:333` (`load_compositions(xlsx_path)`). openpyxl is not in pyproject.toml dependencies.

## Symptom

Swarm benchmark gate dispatches on vasp-03 fail at `load_default_datasets()` with:
```
ImportError: openpyxl is required to load Vrábel2020 support tables.
Install with: uv pip install openpyxl
```

Failure mode: 130s gate timeout, status=failed, no PR opened. Diagnosed live during heavy-tier dispatch at 22:34 UTC on CF-LIBS-improved-p45k.

## Fix

Add `openpyxl>=3.0` to pyproject.toml `[project.dependencies]` (or the appropriate optional-dep group like 'datasets'). Workaround applied tonight: manually `pip install openpyxl` on vasp-03 venv. That fix won't survive a fresh venv rebuild.

## Acceptance

- `uv pip install -e .` on a fresh venv installs openpyxl
- benchmark gate dispatch on a clean machine succeeds past load_default_datasets()
close_reason: Off-task per refocus 2026-05-08 22:55: loop's sole goal is peak/line ID + CF-LIBS analysis pipeline

title: Bayesian composition workflow produces empty predictions (aitchison=None, predicted_composition=[])
description:
## Bug

The `_fit_bayesian_pipeline()` function in `cflibs/benchmark/unified.py` (added in PR #96) runs end-to-end on GPU successfully but emits CompositionEvaluationRecord with:
- `aitchison: None`
- `predicted_composition: []` (empty)
- `closure_residual: None`
- only `true_composition` populated

Verified by inspecting `/scratch/cf-libs-bench/repo/output/jax-smoke-1754/composition_records.json` after Agent C's smoke run produced 24 records with all fields above being None/empty.

## Root cause hypothesis

The NumPyro NUTS sampler runs successfully (97% GPU util observed for 5+ minutes), but `_fit_bayesian_pipeline()` doesn't pull the posterior-mean composition out of the MCMC samples back into the prediction dict. Likely the function returns the sample dict but never computes `mean(samples['concentrations'], axis=0)` and writes it to `predicted_composition`.

## Fix

In `cflibs/benchmark/unified.py::_fit_bayesian_pipeline`, after `nuts_kernel.run(...)`, extract:

```python
samples = mcmc.get_samples()
posterior_mean_comp = {elem: float(samples['concentrations'][:, i].mean()) for i, elem in enumerate(elements)}
```

Then populate the CompositionEvaluationRecord's `predicted_composition` field. Also compute aitchison_distance(true_comp, posterior_mean_comp) and populate `aitchison` field.

## Acceptance

- New jax-smoke run with --composition-workflows bayesian produces composition_records.json where >=80% of records have non-None aitchison + non-empty predicted_composition
- Sample d_A values are in physical range (0-20 on the simplex scale)
- 11 existing tests in tests/benchmark/test_jax_workflows.py still pass

## Why P1

The d_A delta gate's signal depends on aitchison values being populated. Without this fix, the bayesian workflow can't contribute to the optimization signal — every gate run with bayesian workflow emits records with verdict='no_records' (empty grouped dict).

title: Cut Aitchison distance on NIST steel SRMs via Aki-uncertainty-weighted Boltzmann fit
description:
## Goal
Reduce composition error on NIST steel SRMs (1261a-1265a) by switching from uniform-weighted to inverse-variance-weighted (1/σ²(A_ki)) Boltzmann fitting. Per Kramida 2024 + the CF-LIBS notebook.

## Why
NIST atomic data has heavy heteroscedasticity in A_ki uncertainties — accuracy grades A→E correspond to σ from 0.3% to 50%. Uniform OLS fitting in cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py treats all transitions equally, biasing T-estimate toward poorly-known lines. Inverse-variance weighting via Kramida 2024 σ(A_ki) values (already integrated in PR #71) is mathematically correct.

## Target
- cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py — add aki_uncertainty_weighting kwarg (already in cflibs/inversion/physics/boltzmann.py from PR #81; backport to numpy version)
- cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:1158 iterative_configs — add weighting='aki_inverse_variance'

## Acceptance
- d_A delta on NIST steel adapter shows verdict='improved' (delta_rel <= -5%, delta_abs <= -0.005)
- Per-element MAE on Fe/Cr/Ni/Mn improves >= 10% relative
- Aalto + USGS no regression

## Datasets
- NIST steel via cflibs/benchmark/datasets/nist_steel.py
- Aalto + USGS regression guards

title: Improve plasma T estimate on Aalto via two-region (core+corona) fit
description:
## Goal
Tune plasma temperature inference on the Aalto mineral benchmark using a two-region (Hermann core+corona) fit.

## Why
Aalto majors (Si/Fe/Ca/Al/Mg) currently fit single-zone LTE plasma. Real LIBS plasmas are stratified: hot core + cooler corona. Modeling both regions reduces T-estimate bias for high-Z elements emitting from outer shells. Per Hermann 2017, this is a 5-15% accuracy improvement for matrix elements.

## Target
- cflibs/plasma/state.py — extend SingleZoneLTEPlasma to support TwoRegionPlasma
- cflibs/inversion/solver.py IterativeCFLIBSSolver — accept two-region temperature pair
- cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:1158 iterative_configs — add two-region variant

## Acceptance
- d_A delta verdict='improved' OR 'flat' on Aalto (relative <= -2%, abs <= -0.001)
- T-estimates remain in 5000-20000 K physical range (per protocol.yaml plasma_t_physicality)
- LTE / McWhirter / closure gates still pass

## Datasets
- Aalto (primary) via cflibs/benchmark/datasets/...
- Cross-dataset regression guards on USGS + NIST steel

title: Reduce closure residual on USGS BCR-2 via Dirichlet-residual closure with γ_residual
description:
## Goal
Fit USGS BCR-2 (Columbia River basalt) compositions using the Dirichlet-Residual closure (γ_residual latent parameter) to absorb VUV-blind systematic error.

## Why
Per the CF-LIBS notebook (NotebookLM 9170d55e): rigid sum(C)=1 closure inflates detected elements when major emitters fall outside the spectrometer range (halogens/S/P in VUV). BCR-2 has known traces of Cl/S that current pipeline mis-handles. Dirichlet-residual with latent γ decouples trace recovery from bulk completeness — a Tier-2 protocol.yaml-spec'd improvement.

## Target
- cflibs/inversion/closure.py — add DirichletResidualClosure mode (existing has 'standard', 'ilr', 'pwlr')
- cflibs/inversion/physics/quality.py — extend ClosureQualityMetrics with gamma_residual field
- cflibs/benchmark/unified.py iterative_configs — add closure_mode='dirichlet_residual'

## Acceptance
- d_A delta on BCR-2 (USGS adapter) shows verdict='improved' OR 'flat'
- closure_residual metric stays < 0.10 (protocol.yaml threshold)
- gamma_residual reported in composition_records.json annotations

## Datasets
- USGS BCR-2 via cflibs/benchmark/datasets/usgs.py
- Aalto + Vrabel2020 regression guards

title: Cut Aitchison distance on Vrabel2020 soil benchmark via self-absorption correction
description:
## Goal
Reduce composition error on the Vrabel2020 LIBS soil benchmark (50K spectra, 12 soil classes, 138 USGS-CRM samples) by tuning self-absorption correction for the matrix.

## Why
Vrabel2020 (data/vrabel2020_soil_benchmark/, just shipped in PR #86 via cflibs/pds/vrabel2020.py) is the largest and most heterogeneous real-data benchmark we have. Self-absorption is the dominant systematic for major-element emission lines in soil matrices (high Si, Al, Fe). cflibs/inversion/physics/cdsb.py implements Column-Density Saha-Boltzmann (CD-SB) but has tunable convergence parameters.

## Target
- cflibs/inversion/physics/cdsb.py — n_iterations + convergence tolerance + initial column density estimate
- scripts/run_vrabel2020_benchmark.py — composition mode acceptance thresholds
- Optionally: extend cflibs/benchmark/unified.py to register a 'vrabel2020' dataset adapter

## Acceptance
- Median d_A on Vrabel2020 composition mode reduced by >= 10% vs current floor
- Aitchison distance per-class on majors (Si/Al/Fe/Ca/Mg/K/Na) stays in 'good' tier (< 0.10) for at least 8 of 12 classes
- Aalto + USGS no regression (delta_rel <= +5% on each)

## Datasets
- Vrabel2020 (primary): data/vrabel2020_soil_benchmark/{train,test}.h5
- Aalto + USGS (regression guards)

title: Improve peak-ID recall on USGS BHVO-2 trace elements via comb_identifier threshold tuning
description:
## Goal
Push per-element recall on USGS reference materials (BHVO-2 Hawaiian basalt, AGV-2 andesite, BCR-2 Columbia River basalt, G-2 granite) trace-element identification.

## Why
Trace-element peak-ID drives the LIBS algorithm's scientific value — major-element ID is solved; the open problem is recovering V/Cr/Ni/Co/Sr/Y/Zr at <0.1 wt% in basalt matrices. cflibs/inversion/comb_identifier.py implements comb-correlation against NIST line tables but uses fixed thresholds that may over-reject low-SNR detections.

## Target
- cflibs/inversion/comb_identifier.py — score threshold + minimum-line-count parameters
- cflibs/benchmark/datasets/usgs.py — confirm the spectrum loader exposes traces correctly
- protocol.yaml validation/protocol.yaml identification.recall_floor_per_element

## Acceptance
- Per-element recall >= 0.6 for V/Cr/Ni on at least one of {BHVO-2, BCR-2}
- macro_F1 regression on Aalto majors <= 0.02 absolute (no negative transfer)
- d_A delta verdict 'flat' or 'improved' (no compositional regression)

## Datasets
- USGS via cflibs/benchmark/datasets/usgs.py (BHVO-2, AGV-2, BCR-2, G-2)
- Aalto cross-dataset generalization guard (no regression)

title: Reduce Aitchison distance on Aalto via Boltzmann outlier rejection tuning
description:
## Goal
Improve composition-accuracy on the Aalto mineral benchmark by tuning the Boltzmann fitter's outlier rejection.

## Why
The d_A delta gate now measures composition accuracy (Milestone A, PR #260). The Aalto dataset (cflibs/benchmark/datasets/...) is the primary mineral-matrix benchmark. Boltzmann plot residuals dominate composition error when major-line emission lines have detector-noise outliers (Cristoforetti 2010).

## Target
- Modify cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py and/or cflibs/inversion/physics/boltzmann.py outlier-sigma + max-iterations defaults
- Optionally tune cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:1158 iterative_configs to use stricter SIGMA_CLIP

## Acceptance
- d_A delta gate verdict='improved' on the post-merge benchmark vs last_green_main baseline (relative <= -5%, absolute <= -0.005)
- All 6 pipelines (HUBER/RANSAC/SIGMA_CLIP × {standard,ilr}) maintain n_succeeded >= 90%
- No physical-consistency tier-1 trip (LTE / McWhirter / closure / T-physicality)

## Datasets
- Aalto via existing benchmark adapter
- Validates against composition_records.json -> cross_dataset_median_d_a

title: [element-id] fix: RP estimator -- sub-pixel FWHM and remove hardcoded 300 floor
description:
## What's wrong

`_estimate_effective_rp` in `scripts/calibrate_alias.py:235-260` walks
integer indices outward from each peak until intensity drops below
half-max:

```python
left = p
while left > 0 and spectrum[left] > half_max:
    left -= 1
right = p
while right < len(spectrum) - 1 and spectrum[right] > half_max:
    right += 1
fwhm = wavelength[right] - wavelength[left]
if 0.05 < fwhm < 3.0 and wavelength[p] > 0:
    fwhm_rps.append(wavelength[p] / fwhm)
...
return float(np.clip(np.median(fwhm_rps), 300.0, 20000.0))
```

Two problems:

1. **Pixel-granular FWHM.** On a typical Aalto grid (Δλ ≈ 0.1 nm), the
   smallest measurable FWHM is ~1 pixel = ~0.1 nm. At λ=550 nm that
   caps RP at ~5500 with an artificial granularity. Real RP ≈ 600
   spectra get FWHM ≈ 0.92 nm, still on-grid, but RP ≈ 5000 spectra
   get under-sampled and either return 0.1 (rejected by the 0.05
   floor) or jump to the 3.0 nm filter ceiling and get rejected too.

2. **Hardcoded 300 floor and 20000 ceiling.** Real RP < 300 spectra
   are silently clipped to RP = 300 — but those spectra exist (some
   compact spectrometers run < 300 in the IR). The clipping inflates
   the apparent RP and causes the unified runner to pick a basis
   FWHM that's too narrow.

3. **Bias from blends.** No filter excludes blended peaks before
   FWHM measurement. A wide blend looks like a wide peak, lowering
   estimated RP. The `np.percentile(spectrum, 85)` peak threshold is
   noise-blind too.

## What to build

1. Replace the integer-index walk with `scipy.signal.peak_widths`:

   ```python
   from scipy.signal import find_peaks, peak_widths

   def _estimate_effective_rp(wavelength, spectrum, return_diagnostic=False):
       baseline = ...  # use existing estimate_baseline if available
       corrected = np.maximum(spectrum - baseline, 0.0)
       noise = ...  # use estimate_noise

       # Peaks above 5x noise, well separated
       peaks, props = find_peaks(
           corrected,
           height=5.0 * noise,
           distance=int(round(0.5 / np.median(np.diff(wavelength)))),  # ~0.5 nm
           prominence=3.0 * noise,
       )
       if len(peaks) < 5:
           return None  # no estimate (caller decides fallback)

       # Sub-pixel FWHM via linear interpolation at half-max
       widths_pix, _, left_ips, right_ips = peak_widths(corrected, peaks, rel_height=0.5)

       # Convert pixel widths to wavelength widths
       grid = np.asarray(wavelength)
       def interp(idx):
           lo = int(np.floor(idx)); hi = min(lo + 1, len(grid) - 1)
           frac = idx - lo
           return grid[lo] * (1 - frac) + grid[hi] * frac
       fwhm_nm = np.array([interp(r) - interp(l) for l, r in zip(left_ips, right_ips)])
       valid = fwhm_nm > 2 * np.median(np.diff(grid))  # > 2 pixels
       if valid.sum() < 5:
           return None

       rp = grid[peaks[valid]] / fwhm_nm[valid]
       # Robust median, no hardcoded clip
       return float(np.median(rp))
   ```

2. Remove the hardcoded `np.clip(..., 300, 20000)` floor and ceiling.
   Callers that need a fallback for `None` (small-peak-count case)
   should handle it explicitly:
   - In `_estimate_rp_for_spectrum` (`cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:759-762`):
     wrap with `or DEFAULT_RP` where `DEFAULT_RP = 1000.0`.

3. Optional but recommended: use sigma-clipped MAD on the per-peak
   `rp` array to reject outliers before taking the median.

4. Backward compatibility: keep the public function signature; add a
   `min_peaks: int = 5` kwarg with default 5 (was 3).

## Tests

- `tests/scripts/test_estimate_effective_rp.py` (new):
  - Synthesize a spectrum with known FWHM=0.5 nm at λ ≈ 500 nm.
    Verify estimated RP ≈ 1000 (within 10%).
  - FWHM=1.5 nm → RP ≈ 333. Verify NOT clipped to 300.
  - Spectrum with 3 peaks only → returns None (or fallback at the
    caller level).
  - Spectrum with all blended peaks → none pass the 2-pixel filter,
    returns None.

- `tests/benchmark/test_rp_distribution.py` (new, requires Aalto):
  - Run `_estimate_effective_rp` on all 74 Aalto spectra. Verify
    estimated RP histogram is broader than the published 300–1100
    range (we expect values down to ~250 and up to ~1500).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `_estimate_effective_rp` rewritten using `scipy.signal.peak_widths`
      with sub-pixel interpolation.
- [ ] Hardcoded `300` floor and `20000` ceiling removed; callers
      handle `None` explicitly.
- [ ] New tests in `tests/scripts/test_estimate_effective_rp.py` pass.
- [ ] Re-run unified benchmark; attached JSON includes per-spectrum
      `rp_estimate` values that differ from the previously published
      values for at least 30% of the corpus (showing the estimator
      actually changed).
- [ ] No existing test in `tests/` regresses. If a downstream test
      relied on RP=300 floor, fix the test.

## Out of scope

- Wavelength calibration (auto-shift).
- Per-region RP variation (echelle spectrometers have wavelength-dependent
  RP). This epic estimates a single global RP per spectrum.
design: Replace the integer-index walk with scipy.signal.peak_widths sub-pixel interpolation; remove hardcoded clip; let callers handle the no-estimate case.
acceptance_criteria: See description. Tests pass; benchmark JSON shows rp_estimate values changed for >=30% of Aalto spectra.
notes:
Parent epic: CF-LIBS-improved-340. File hot spot: scripts/calibrate_alias.py:235-260. Caller fallback: cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:759-762.
[dispatch-guard] kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 2 target(s) missing: tests/scripts/test_estimate_effective_rp.py, tests/benchmark/test_rp_distribution.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).

title: [element-id] feature: wire FAISS BasisIndex for data-driven (T, n_e) in NNLS
description:
## What's wrong

Every NNLS predictor in the unified benchmark constructs the basis
matrix at a static fallback `(T, n_e)`, ignoring the data:

- `_build_nnls_predictor` (`cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:846-868`)
  passes only `fallback_T_K` (8000-10000), `fallback_ne_cm3=1e17`.
- `SpectralNNLSIdentifier` itself only consults `basis_index`
  when one is provided (`cflibs/inversion/identify/spectral_nnls.py:153-159`).
  None of the predictor builders construct one.

The basis library already supports bilinear interpolation in (T, n_e),
and `cflibs/manifold/basis_index.py` (or equivalent) implements a
FAISS nearest-neighbor query over the (T, n_e) grid. We have the
infrastructure — it's not wired.

For ps-LIBS plasma temperatures (0.5–1.3 eV, 5800–15000 K — see
session memory), 8000 K is in-range but not always optimal. Saha-
Boltzmann ionization balance is exponentially sensitive to T near
the ionization threshold, so a 2000 K mismatch shifts ionic-stage
populations by factors of 2–5 for many transitions, distorting the
basis and forcing NNLS coefficients to compensate.

## What to build

1. **Build the index per basis FWHM**:

   ```
   python scripts/build_basis_index.py \
       --basis output/basis_libraries/basis_fwhm_<X>nm.h5 \
       --output output/basis_libraries/basis_fwhm_<X>nm.faiss
   ```

   If `scripts/build_basis_index.py` doesn't exist, write it.
   Implementation notes:
   - Each grid point (T_i, n_e_j) has a normalized basis-sum
     spectrum (sum over elements). Index those vectors.
   - Use `IndexFlatL2` (300 grid points × 8 FWHMs is small).
   - Persist alongside the .h5 with the same stem and `.faiss` suffix.

2. **Wire into UnifiedBenchmarkContext**:

   Augment `basis_for_rp()` to return `(library, fwhm, mismatch, index)`,
   loading the matching `.faiss` file lazily.

3. **Wire into the predictors**:

   `_build_nnls_predictor` and `_build_hybrid_predictor` construct
   `SpectralNNLSIdentifier(basis_library=..., basis_index=index, ...)`.
   With an index present, `identify()` already calls
   `basis_index.estimate_plasma_params(observed_resampled, k=50)`.

4. **Document T/n_e estimate in the result**:

   The result already reports `estimated_T_K` and `estimated_ne_cm3`.
   Verify they're populated when `basis_index` is provided.

## Tests

- `tests/inversion/identify/test_nnls_with_basis_index.py` (new):
  - Synthesize spectrum at known (T=10000, n_e=3e16). Run NNLS with
    a basis index. Assert estimated T within 1500 K, estimated n_e
    within 0.5 dex.
  - Compare detection results to the same NNLS without index but
    with `fallback_T_K=8000`. Detection set should agree on the
    high-confidence elements; coefficient ordering should be similar.

- `tests/benchmark/test_basis_index_load.py` (new, requires basis):
  - Loads an index from disk, runs one inference, asserts shape +
    return values.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `output/basis_libraries/basis_fwhm_<X>nm.faiss` exists for each
      FWHM in the ladder (depends on epic CF-LIBS-improved-i7f).
- [ ] `scripts/build_basis_index.py` (new or updated) produces them.
- [ ] `UnifiedBenchmarkContext.basis_for_rp` loads + caches the index.
- [ ] `_build_nnls_predictor`, `_build_nnls_concentration_predictor`,
      and `_build_hybrid_predictor` all pass `basis_index=` to
      `SpectralNNLSIdentifier`.
- [ ] New tests pass.
- [ ] Re-run unified benchmark on Aalto corpus; benchmark JSON shows
      varying `estimated_T_K` per spectrum (not constant 8000 / 10000).

## Dependencies

- Blocked by CF-LIBS-improved-i7f (multi-FWHM basis ladder).
- Soft dependency on CF-LIBS-improved-814 (basis filter): when both
  ship together, the index lookup operates on the filtered subset
  consistently.

## Out of scope

- Joint MCMC marginalization over (T, n_e) — that lives in
  CF-LIBS-improved-cci (NumPyro plasma-param marginalization).
design: Build one FAISS IndexFlatL2 per basis FWHM and load it in UnifiedBenchmarkContext.basis_for_rp; pass through to SpectralNNLSIdentifier and HybridIdentifier.
acceptance_criteria: See description. Benchmark JSON shows per-spectrum varying T estimate; new tests pass; FAISS .faiss files exist alongside basis ladder.
notes: Parent epic: CF-LIBS-improved-340. Depends on i7f (multi-FWHM ladder). File hot spots: cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:846-897; cflibs/inversion/identify/spectral_nnls.py:153-159; cflibs/manifold/basis_index.py.

title: [element-id] fix: Mn/Na/line-rich structure gates at RP<1500
description:
## What's wrong

Mn and Na (and to a lesser extent Fe/Cr/Ti) are pathological
false-positive sources at RP < 1000 across all algorithms tested in
the paper. Per-element table from `docs/reports/element_id_benchmark_report.md`:

- Mn: P=0.03, R=1.00 — every spectrum gets an Mn FP.
- Na: P=0.14, R=0.80 — same story.

This is a real physics constraint: at RP=600, Mn's >500 catalogued
transitions in 200–900 nm form a near-continuous comb that overlaps
with any peak set. The paper acknowledges this and recommends
"resolving-power-aware rules for known pathological elements".

The existing iron-group pre-subtraction at
`cflibs/inversion/identify/alias.py:364-403` only fires when
`self.resolving_power < 2000` AND it lowers `P_local` for *non*-iron-
group elements. It never penalizes Mn / Fe / Cr / Ti themselves.

## What to build

A two-part suppression for line-rich species at low RP:

### Part A — Multi-line ratio test (Mn 403-nm triplet)

The Mn I 403 nm triplet (403.076, 403.307, 403.449 nm) is the
characteristic Mn signature visible at RP = 600–1000. If Mn is truly
present, all three lines should appear with intensity ratios
consistent with their gA values. If Mn is being detected from chance
matches, the triplet structure will be absent.

Implement a generic `multi_line_ratio_score(element, fused_lines, ...)`
in `cflibs/inversion/identify/alias.py` that:

1. Selects the top-3 strongest lines (by predicted emissivity at the
   estimated T) of the candidate element within a configurable
   wavelength region.
2. Predicts their intensity ratios from atomic data (gA times
   Boltzmann).
3. Measures the observed ratios from matched-peak intensities.
4. Returns a score in [0, 1] based on log-ratio agreement (use the
   geometric-mean residual of |log(observed/predicted)| capped at,
   e.g., a factor of 5×).

Per-element configuration in a new dict
`STRUCTURE_TEST_REGIONS` (top of `alias.py`):

```python
STRUCTURE_TEST_REGIONS = {
    "Mn": (402.0, 405.0),  # Mn I triplet
    "Fe": (370.0, 405.0),  # Fe I forest
    "Cr": (425.0, 430.0),  # Cr I triplet
    "Ti": (498.0, 502.0),  # Ti II 4p multiplet
    # Na: skip — only 2 useful lines (D-doublet) so structure test is moot;
    # Na gets a separate D-line ratio check (see Part B).
}
```

Apply the score as a multiplicative gate on CL when
`self.resolving_power < 1500` and `element in STRUCTURE_TEST_REGIONS`.

### Part B — Na D-line ratio sanity check

Na has the 589.0/589.6 nm D-doublet. Their predicted intensity ratio
is gA(D2)/gA(D1) = 2 (D2 is twice as strong as D1). If only one of
the doublet members is matched, or the observed ratio differs from
predicted by > 3x in either direction, reject Na.

Add a dedicated `_na_doublet_check()` method in
`alias.py` that runs only when `element == "Na"` and `resolving_power < 1500`.
Returns a CL multiplier in [0, 1].

### Part C — Optional: line-density penalty

For elements with >100 catalogued transitions in [200, 900] nm at
the basis emissivity threshold, scale the chance-coincidence
significance by a "comb redundancy" factor: estimate the fraction
of the spectrum's wavelength axis covered by the element's combined
emission profile (sum of Gaussian wings at instrumental FWHM). If
that fraction exceeds, e.g., 30%, divide CL by 2 — the element's
spectrum is "everywhere" and its chance match probability is
elevated even after the binomial correction.

This is optional (best validated empirically post-fix). Wire it
behind a `comb_redundancy_penalty: bool = True` constructor kwarg.

## Tests

- `tests/inversion/identify/test_mn_triplet_gate.py` (new):
  - Build a synthetic spectrum WITH Mn at known T, instrument FWHM
    1.5 nm (RP ≈ 270). Verify Mn is detected with high CL.
  - Build a synthetic spectrum WITHOUT Mn but WITH Fe (which has
    lines near 403 nm). Verify Mn is NOT detected — the structure
    test should reject because Mn's 403 nm peaks come from Fe
    contamination, not from a true Mn triplet.

- `tests/inversion/identify/test_na_doublet_gate.py` (new):
  - Synthetic with only Na D2 (589.0) but no D1 → reject.
  - Synthetic with both lines but ratio inverted (D1 > D2) → reject.
  - Synthetic with both lines, predicted ratio → accept.

- `tests/benchmark/test_aalto_mn_na_drop.py` (new, marked
  `@pytest.mark.requires_aalto_data`):
  - Run the hybrid identifier on the 13 pure-element spectra.
  - Verify Mn FP count on the 12 non-Mn pure-element spectra drops
    from currently ~10 to <= 2.
  - Verify Na FP count drops likewise.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `multi_line_ratio_score()` and `STRUCTURE_TEST_REGIONS`
      implemented in `cflibs/inversion/identify/alias.py`.
- [ ] `_na_doublet_check()` implemented and applied for Na at low RP.
- [ ] Both gates fire only when `self.resolving_power < 1500`.
- [ ] All three new test files pass.
- [ ] Per-element scorecard from
      `cflibs/benchmark/unified.py` shows: Mn precision rises from
      0.03 → at least 0.4; Na precision rises from 0.14 → at least
      0.4. Recall on Mn / Na for spectra that genuinely contain them
      (mntantalite, plagioclase, scapolite, tourmaline) does NOT drop
      below 0.5. Numbers attached as JSON in PR.

## Out of scope

- Fe / Cr / Ti structure tests beyond the regions table; if those
  prove insufficient, file a follow-up bug.
- Changing the chance_window_scale default.
design: Add a multi-line ratio test for Mn (403 nm triplet), Cr (425-430), Ti, Fe; plus a dedicated Na D-doublet ratio check. Both gate CL multiplicatively only at RP<1500. Validate against true Mn/Na minerals.
acceptance_criteria: See description. Mn precision >= 0.4, Na precision >= 0.4 on Aalto corpus, without losing recall on Mn/Na-bearing minerals.
notes: Parent epic: CF-LIBS-improved-340. Best validated AFTER epics 1-3 land — current Mn/Na FPs are inflated by NNLS soup and basis mismatch. File hot spots: cflibs/inversion/identify/alias.py:364-403 (existing iron-group hook); _decide() at line ~2150.
close_reason: stale-target (preflight): kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 4 target(s) missing: alias.py, tests/inversion/identify/test_mn_triplet_gate.py, tests/inversion/identify/test_na_doublet_gate.py, tests/benchmark/test_aalto_mn_na_drop.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).

title: Audit atomic database coverage for Aalto search elements
description: Production atomic DB is missing Zr, while Zr is in the Aalto search/label universe. Several elements log missing/approximated higher-stage energy levels during ALIAS/hybrid runs. Audit which benchmark labels are impossible or weakly modeled, add missing species data where feasible, or make benchmark reports stratify unsupported elements explicitly.

title: Suppress line-rich false positives in low-RP element ID
description: Benchmark diagnostics show hybrid best config still dominated by false positives: Mn 29, Na 21, Mg 16, plus K. Investigate RP-dependent element-specific thresholds/post-filters, Na D doublet confirmation, Mn multiplet/line-density penalties, and learned physics-feature post-classifier trained on synthetic spectra. Verify on pure-element spectra where Co, Fe, Ni, and V currently get contaminant detections.

title: [element-id] data: generate multi-FWHM basis library ladder for low-RP coverage
description:
## What's wrong

The unified runner has the right code path for picking a basis library
matched to the spectrum's RP — `UnifiedBenchmarkContext.basis_for_rp`
in `cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:623-635` selects the basis whose FWHM
is closest to `550 / RP_estimate`. But the directory it scans
(`output/basis_libraries/`) does not exist in the repo, so the
benchmark falls back to a single 0.5-nm-FWHM basis library. From the
paper Table 1: "Nominal Instrument FWHM 0.5 nm".

For Aalto spectra at RP=300 (the lower edge of the 300–1100 range),
the actual instrument FWHM at lambda=550 nm is 1.83 nm — 3.7× wider
than the basis lines. NNLS sees observed broad blends but basis
spectra with sharp lines: it will spread coefficient mass across
many elements to reproduce a single broad envelope, and ALIAS sees
no isolated peaks.

This is the obvious physical mismatch behind the published
low-RP failure mode.

## What to build

1. Run `scripts/hpc/generate_basis_libraries.py` for the full FWHM
   ladder already declared in that script
   (`FWHM_VALUES = [0.05, 0.10, 0.17, 0.25, 0.50, 0.71, 1.00, 1.67]`).
   The output goes in `output/basis_libraries/`.
   Naming convention required by `basis_for_rp`:
   `basis_fwhm_<value>nm.h5` (regex
   `r"basis_fwhm_([0-9.]+)nm\.h5$"`). The HPC script writes
   `basis_library_fwhm_<value>.h5`. **Reconcile naming**:
   - Easiest: edit `_output_filename` in
     `scripts/hpc/generate_basis_libraries.py:55-57` to emit
     `basis_fwhm_{fwhm:.2f}nm.h5`.
   - OR rename the regex in `unified.py:617` to match. Either is fine;
     pick one and update both call sites.

2. Generate locally where feasible (Apple Silicon, JAX Metal):
   ```
   python scripts/hpc/generate_basis_libraries.py \
       --db ASD_da/libs_production.db \
       --output-dir output/basis_libraries \
       --fwhm 0.05 0.10 0.17 0.25 0.50 0.71 1.00 1.67
   ```
   If too slow on local hardware, document the SLURM submission
   command (`--mode submit`).

3. The basis grids inherit from `_nnls_workflow_configs` defaults;
   no change to T/n_e grids needed for this epic.

4. Update `paper/low_resolution_element_id/main.tex` Table 1 caption
   to say "instrument FWHM matched per spectrum from basis ladder
   {0.05, 0.10, 0.17, 0.25, 0.50, 0.71, 1.00, 1.67} nm" instead of
   the fixed 0.5 nm. Do this in the same PR that re-runs the
   benchmark.

## Tests

- `tests/benchmark/test_basis_for_rp.py` (new):
  - Mock a `basis_dir` with empty files at all 8 FWHM values.
  - For RP=300 (target FWHM ≈ 1.83 nm), assert `basis_for_rp(300)`
    returns the 1.67 nm file (closest to 1.83 nm).
  - For RP=1100 (target FWHM ≈ 0.5), assert it returns 0.50.
  - For RP=None or RP=0, it must fall back to 0.5 (current behavior).

- `tests/benchmark/test_basis_libraries_present.py` (new, marked
  `@pytest.mark.requires_basis_libraries`):
  - Assert `output/basis_libraries/` contains 8 files matching the
    ladder. Skip if the dir doesn't exist (so CI without the data
    doesn't fail; gate is wired in nightly CI).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All 8 basis libraries exist under `output/basis_libraries/` on
      a target machine (local or HPC). Filenames match
      `basis_fwhm_<X.XX>nm.h5`.
- [ ] `_output_filename` in `scripts/hpc/generate_basis_libraries.py`
      is reconciled with the regex in `unified.py:617`.
- [ ] `tests/benchmark/test_basis_for_rp.py` passes.
- [ ] `scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py --id-workflows spectral_nnls
      hybrid_intersect` runs to completion using the multi-FWHM ladder.
      Attached JSON shows `basis_fwhm_nm` varies across spectra.
- [ ] Paper Table 1 caption updated, paper rebuilt
      (`paper/low_resolution_element_id/main.pdf`).

## Out of scope

- FAISS basis index construction is its own epic (#5).
- Increasing the (T, n_e) grid resolution. Re-use the current
  30 × 10 grid.
design: Run the existing scripts/hpc/generate_basis_libraries.py for the [0.05, 0.10, 0.17, 0.25, 0.50, 0.71, 1.00, 1.67] ladder. Reconcile filename convention between the generator and the unified-runner regex.
acceptance_criteria: See description. 8 basis_fwhm_*.h5 files materialized, basis_for_rp picks per spectrum, benchmark JSON shows variation.
notes: Parent epic: CF-LIBS-improved-340. File hot spots: scripts/hpc/generate_basis_libraries.py:55-57; cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:617. Generation is expensive — run on cluster if too slow locally.
close_reason: stale-target (preflight): kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 3 target(s) missing: unified.py, tests/benchmark/test_basis_for_rp.py, tests/benchmark/test_basis_libraries_present.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).

title: Add resolution-matched basis selection for element ID benchmark
description: The Aalto benchmark spans effective RP about 300-1100, but the NNLS/hybrid runs use a single 0.5 nm basis. At RP 300, FWHM is roughly 1.7 nm near 500 nm, so the basis is over-resolved. Implement/generate multiple FWHM basis libraries and select per spectrum by measured RP or measured FWHM; rerun benchmark with full hyperparameter sweep.

title: [element-id] fix: replace NNLS 1e-4 signal-var noise floor with per-pixel MAD noise
description:
## What's wrong

The NNLS coefficient SNR is computed against a noise floor that is
defined as `1e-4 * signal_var`. After area-normalization (which the
identifier does at line 148-150), `signal_var` is dominated by a
handful of bright peaks, so `1e-4 * signal_var` is a tiny number.
Every basis column with a nonzero coefficient ends up at SNR ≫ 3 and
the SNR threshold is uninformative.

**Concrete reference**: `cflibs/inversion/identify/spectral_nnls.py:181-198`

```python
residual_var = float(np.sum(residual**2) / dof) if len(residual) > 0 else 1e-20
# Use a realistic noise floor: 1% of the signal variance
signal_var = float(np.var(observed_resampled)) if len(observed_resampled) > 0 else 1e-20
noise_floor = max(signal_var * 1e-4, 1e-20)   # comment says 1%, code says 0.01%
residual_var = max(residual_var, noise_floor)
...
sigma_coeffs = np.sqrt(np.maximum(residual_var * AtA_inv_diag[:n_elements], 0.0))
snr = element_coeffs / np.maximum(sigma_coeffs, 1e-10)
```

The comment is wrong (says 1%, code is 0.01%), but the floor is the
wrong concept anyway: the right noise statistic for a coefficient
covariance is the per-pixel measurement noise, not a fraction of the
total signal variance.

## Why this matters

After fix #1 (epic CF-LIBS-improved-814), the basis is the right
size, but spurious detections still pass because `sigma_coeffs` is
too small. The published P=0.293 / R=0.940 NNLS-only point is
exactly what you get when the SNR threshold is effectively
disabled.

We already have a proper noise estimator —
`cflibs/inversion/preprocess/preprocessing.py:266` `estimate_noise()`
returns sigma-clipped MAD noise on the *raw* spectrum. We need to
propagate that estimate through the resampling and area-normalization
to the library grid, then use it as the per-pixel sigma.

## What to build

1. In `cflibs/inversion/identify/spectral_nnls.py:identify()`:

   - After `baseline = estimate_baseline(...)`:
     ```python
     pixel_noise_raw = estimate_noise(intensity, baseline)
     ```
   - After resampling+area-normalization, compute the equivalent
     noise on the library grid:
     ```python
     # Resample noise level to library grid (constant noise -> constant)
     # then divide by the same area used to normalize the signal.
     pixel_noise_lib = pixel_noise_raw / max(area, 1e-20)
     ```
     (Justification: area-normalization is a scalar division; if raw
     noise sigma is sigma_raw per pixel and signal is divided by area,
     then noise sigma on the normalized grid is sigma_raw/area.)

   - Use `pixel_noise_lib**2` as the residual variance:
     ```python
     residual_var = float(pixel_noise_lib**2)
     # Optional: rescale by reduced chi-squared if fit is poor
     chi2_red = float(np.sum(residual**2) / max(dof * residual_var, 1e-20))
     if chi2_red > 1.0:
         residual_var *= chi2_red
     ```

   - Drop the `signal_var * 1e-4` floor entirely.

2. Document the change in the SpectralNNLSIdentifier docstring:
   "noise_floor parameter is removed; the identifier uses the
   sigma-clipped MAD per-pixel noise from preprocessing."

## Tests

- `tests/inversion/identify/test_spectral_nnls_noise.py` (new):
  - Synthesize a 4-element spectrum (Fe, Si, Mg, Ca only) with known
    Gaussian noise sigma=N. Use elements=['Fe','Si','Mg','Ca','Mn'].
  - Verify: Mn is rejected (coefficient ~0, SNR < 3). Fe/Si pass.
    Adding noise pushes SNR down by sqrt(N) factor as expected.
  - Compare to the OLD code: with the 1e-4 noise floor, Mn would have
    been "detected" with SNR > 3 even though its true coefficient is 0.

- `tests/inversion/identify/test_nnls_snr_calibration.py` (new):
  - Generate spectra at noise levels [0.001, 0.01, 0.1] of mean
    signal. Confirm SNR for true elements scales as 1/sigma_noise.

- Update `tests/test_integration_element_id.py` if any test relied
  on the old behavior — fix the test, don't suppress the warning.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `noise_floor` heuristic removed from
      `cflibs/inversion/identify/spectral_nnls.py`.
- [ ] Per-pixel noise from `estimate_noise()` is propagated through
      area-normalization to the library grid.
- [ ] New tests pass; existing identifier tests pass.
- [ ] Re-run unified benchmark on Aalto corpus
      (`scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py --id-workflows spectral_nnls`)
      and attach JSON. Expected: NNLS-only precision strictly higher
      than the published 0.293; recall may drop modestly (acceptable
      so long as F1 rises).

## Out of scope

- Don't change the basis filter (epic #1).
- Don't tune the SNR threshold; the existing default is fine once
  the variance is correct. Tunable threshold sweeps live in
  `_nnls_workflow_configs` and remain.
design: Use the existing sigma-clipped MAD noise estimator (estimate_noise) and propagate through area-normalization. Drop the signal-variance heuristic.
acceptance_criteria: See description. NNLS-only precision strictly > 0.293 on Aalto corpus after this fix.

title: [element-id] fix: NNLS basis must honor candidate_elements (filter to scored species)
description:
## What's wrong

`SpectralNNLSIdentifier` ignores the `candidate_elements` parameter
that the benchmark harness passes to it. The basis matrix is built
from `self.basis_library.elements` — the FULL 76-element library,
including O, H, C, N, and ~50 species not in the 22-element scored
set used for the Aalto benchmark.

**Concrete reference**: `cflibs/inversion/identify/spectral_nnls.py:167`

```python
elements = self.basis_library.elements    # all 76, not the scored 22
```

`_build_nnls_predictor` in `cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:846-868`
shoves `candidate_elements` into the result `parameters` dict but
never gates the basis. `_build_hybrid_predictor` (lines 871-897) does
the same.

## Why this is the highest-priority fix

NNLS is a global non-negative active-set solve. Adding 50+ unscored
basis vectors:

1. Lets unscored species (O, H, C, N) absorb genuine spectral content
   that should be attributed to scored majors (Si, Al, Fe, Ca, Mg).
2. Inflates `(A^T A)^{-1}` diagonal entries — unscored line-rich species
   share peaks with scored ones and the resulting near-collinearity
   blows up coefficient uncertainties; in practice this manifests as
   the published P=0.293 / R=0.940 NNLS-only result (everything has
   non-zero coefficient AND inflated SNR thanks to root-cause #2).
3. Breaks the hybrid intersection: `nnls_detected ∩ self.elements`
   silently drops scored TPs because their coefficient was undercut
   by an unscored neighbor.

Expected delta after this fix alone: precision should rise materially
on the hybrid (because spurious NNLS candidates evaporate when the
basis only contains scored species) and recall on Fe/Ca/Mg should
recover a few points each.

## What to build

1. Add `elements: Optional[Sequence[str]] = None` parameter to
   `SpectralNNLSIdentifier.__init__` in
   `cflibs/inversion/identify/spectral_nnls.py`. When provided, it
   filters the basis matrix to those columns.

2. In `identify()`, replace the line
   ```python
   elements = self.basis_library.elements
   basis_matrix = self.basis_library.get_basis_matrix_interp(T_est, ne_est)
   ```
   with
   ```python
   if self.elements is None:
       elements = self.basis_library.elements
       basis_matrix = self.basis_library.get_basis_matrix_interp(T_est, ne_est)
   else:
       full_elems = list(self.basis_library.elements)
       missing = [e for e in self.elements if e not in full_elems]
       if missing:
           raise ValueError(f"Requested elements not in basis: {missing}")
       idx = np.array([full_elems.index(e) for e in self.elements], dtype=int)
       elements = list(self.elements)
       basis_full = self.basis_library.get_basis_matrix_interp(T_est, ne_est)
       basis_matrix = basis_full[idx, :]
   ```

3. Wire `_build_nnls_predictor` and `_build_hybrid_predictor` in
   `cflibs/benchmark/unified.py` to pass `elements=candidate_elements`.

4. Update `HybridIdentifier` in
   `cflibs/inversion/identify/hybrid.py:121-127` to construct
   `SpectralNNLSIdentifier(elements=self.elements, ...)`.

5. Backward compatibility: when `elements is None`, behavior is
   identical to today.

## Tests

- `tests/inversion/identify/test_spectral_nnls_filter.py` (new):
  - Build a tiny 4-element synthetic spectrum (Fe + Si only, area-norm).
  - Run `SpectralNNLSIdentifier` with `elements=['Fe','Si','Mg','Ca']`.
  - Assert basis_matrix shape is (4, n_pixels), result `all_elements`
    has exactly those four entries, and Fe/Si come back detected with
    coefficient SNR > 5 while Mg/Ca are below threshold.
  - Compare against the unfiltered result on the same spectrum: 76
    entries, with O/H absorbing flux and Fe coefficient lower.
- `tests/inversion/identify/test_hybrid_filter.py` (new):
  - Verify `HybridIdentifier(elements=['Fe','Si',...])` produces an
    NNLS stage with exactly those columns. Use a mock or argument
    assertion.
- Existing tests in `tests/test_integration_element_id.py` must
  continue to pass with `elements=None` default.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `SpectralNNLSIdentifier` honors `elements=` parameter.
- [ ] `_build_nnls_predictor`, `_build_nnls_concentration_predictor`,
      `_build_hybrid_predictor` in `cflibs/benchmark/unified.py` pass
      `candidate_elements` through to `SpectralNNLSIdentifier`.
- [ ] `HybridIdentifier` propagates its `elements` list to its
      embedded `SpectralNNLSIdentifier`.
- [ ] New tests in `tests/inversion/identify/test_spectral_nnls_filter.py`
      and `tests/inversion/identify/test_hybrid_filter.py` pass.
- [ ] No existing test in `tests/` regresses.
- [ ] Re-run unified benchmark on Aalto corpus
      (`scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py --id-workflows hybrid_intersect spectral_nnls`)
      and attach JSON deltas to PR. Expected: hybrid F1 strictly higher
      than 0.654, NNLS-only precision strictly higher than 0.293.

## Out of scope

- Don't touch the noise floor (epic #2).
- Don't change basis-library generation (epic #3).
- Don't modify ALIAS scoring.
design: Filter the basis matrix to the scored element subset BEFORE NNLS solve. Backward compatible when elements=None. Touches SpectralNNLSIdentifier, HybridIdentifier, and the three predictor builders in unified.py.
acceptance_criteria: See description. Hybrid F1 strictly > 0.654 after this single fix on the Aalto 74-spectrum corpus.
notes:
Parent epic: CF-LIBS-improved-340. Investigation: CF-LIBS-improved-3zc. File hot spots: cflibs/inversion/identify/spectral_nnls.py:167; cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:846-897; cflibs/inversion/identify/hybrid.py:121-127.

title: [EPIC] element-id: recover lost low-resolution F1 (0.654 -> >=0.80)
description:
The low-resolution element-identification benchmark in
`paper/low_resolution_element_id/main.tex` reports the best hybrid
(NNLS+ALIAS, intersection) at:

- precision 0.604
- recall 0.713
- F₁ 0.654
- false-positive rate 0.053
- 16/74 exact matches

This is unacceptable. Investigation in CF-LIBS-improved-3zc identified
seven concrete root causes in the algorithms and benchmark wiring (NOT
in the underlying physics). Each root cause is captured as its own
PR-scoped child epic below. The expected end state, after all six
fixes land, is F₁ ≥ 0.80 on the same 74-spectrum corpus with the same
hybrid intersection workflow.

## Child epics

1. NNLS basis must honor candidate_elements (filter to scored species)
2. Replace NNLS aggressive noise floor with per-pixel noise estimate
3. Generate multi-FWHM basis library ladder (RP=300–5500 coverage)
4. Mn / Fe / Cr / Ti structure gates at RP<1000 (multi-line ratio test)
5. Wire FAISS BasisIndex for data-driven (T, n_e) in NNLS
6. RP estimator: sub-pixel FWHM + remove hardcoded 300 floor

## Sequencing

- Epics 1, 2, 6 are independent — file in parallel.
- Epic 3 (basis ladder) is independent but expensive; can run in
  parallel.
- Epic 5 (FAISS index) depends on Epic 3 (one index per FWHM).
- Epic 4 (Mn gates) is best validated AFTER Epics 1–3 land, because
  the Mn FP rate is currently inflated by the 76-element NNLS soup.

## Out of scope

- Open PRs #80, #81, #82 are NOT part of this campaign. They are
  worthwhile hygiene but do not move the headline F₁ in this benchmark.
  Merge them on their own merits.
design: Six independent root-cause fixes, each shipped as its own PR. Investigation: CF-LIBS-improved-3zc. Paper artifact: paper/low_resolution_element_id/
acceptance_criteria: Hybrid intersection F1 >= 0.80 on 74-spectrum Aalto corpus at the same operating points as the published paper; 6 child epics closed; benchmark report and paper figures regenerated
notes: Investigation pointer: CF-LIBS-improved-3zc. Open PRs #80/#81/#82 are out of scope.

title: Investigate poor element identification performance
description: Investigate why low-resolution LIBS element identification benchmark performance is poor relative to literature. Examine benchmark labels/scoring, algorithm implementation assumptions, data preprocessing, parameter grids, and potential fixes; record concrete root causes and follow-up work.
notes: Investigation completed: found and fixed benchmark scoring/search-universe inconsistency, added basis subset support and explicit missing-element handling, reproduced ALIAS/hybrid metrics, and filed follow-up issues for resolution matching (CF-LIBS-improved-1ug), false-positive suppression (CF-LIBS-improved-4hj), DB coverage (CF-LIBS-improved-dk8), label quality (CF-LIBS-improved-cly), and ps-LIBS retuning (CF-LIBS-improved-dww).

comments.text: Investigation in progress — completing root-cause analysis on hybrid NNLS+ALIAS benchmark (paper/low_resolution_element_id, F1=0.654).

comments.author: TheFermiSea
comments.text:
Investigation complete. Six PR-scoped epics filed under parent CF-LIBS-improved-340:

  - CF-LIBS-improved-814 — NNLS basis filter (root cause #1, highest impact)
  - CF-LIBS-improved-875 — NNLS noise floor (root cause #2)

  - CF-LIBS-improved-7mm — FAISS basis index (root cause #5; blocked by i7f)

Each epic is self-contained PR work; swarm can pick up. Recommended order: 814 + 875 + i7f + zbu in parallel; then 7mm; then trm validates everything.

title: validation-framework: wire multi-T LTE consistency as Tier-1 hard gate
description:
The benchmark currently logs LTE consistency violations (e.g., the
McWhirter FAIL lines observed during PR #58 nightly). Per
docs/VALIDATION_METRICS.md §2.5, these must be enforced as Tier-1
hard gates: |T_neutral - T_ion| / T_avg < 0.15 (Cristoforetti 2010).

## Why

PR #58 nightly emitted multiple "McWhirter: FAIL n_e = 1.65e+17 <
4.84e+17 cm^-3" lines on real Aalto data. Currently this is info-level
log; the gate doesn't fail. A PR that improves point-estimate accuracy
while violating LTE silently passes.

## What to add

In python/benchmark_gate.py (beefcake-swarm) and/or the comprehensive
benchmark output parser in CF-LIBS-improved:

- Parse the per-spectrum T_neutral, T_ion, n_e, McWhirter floor from
  composition_records.json (or wherever the harness emits them today)
- Compute |T_neutral - T_ion| / T_avg per spectrum
- Aggregate: count of spectra failing each constraint
- Gate logic: any 2 of {LTE consistency, McWhirter floor, T physicality
  bound, closure residual} tripping = block; any 1 = Tier-2 alarm with
  required justification

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Parser unit tests with synthetic harness output
- [ ] Integration: a known-good main commit passes; a known-bad
  injected-fail variant fails the gate
- [ ] PR comment includes per-constraint pass/fail counts
- [ ] Documented in docs/VALIDATION_METRICS.md §2.5 (already specified)
notes: [dispatch-guard] kind:fix requires every target to exist on disk; 1 target(s) missing: python/benchmark_gate.py. If the issue intends to create these files, relabel as kind:create (and a human must authorize that).

title: validation-framework: stratified composition reporting (majors/minors/traces)
description:
Per docs/VALIDATION_METRICS.md §2.1, composition accuracy must be
reported and gated separately for three concentration tiers:
- Majors (> 5 wt%): RD < 5%
- Minors (0.1-5 wt%): RD < 20%
- Traces (< 0.1 wt%): MDL-bounded; report as <= LOQ if below LOQ

## Why

Aggregate per-element metrics let a PR that improves majors at the
cost of trace recovery silently pass — but trace elements (minor
constituents in basalts, alloy steels, geological CRMs) are often
exactly where CF-LIBS adds scientific value. The current benchmark
emits per-element MAE/RMSEP without stratifying by magnitude.

## What to add

Extend cflibs/benchmark/metrics.py BenchmarkMetrics
class to emit a stratified summary alongside the per-element loop:

- per_stratum_summary: {majors, minors, traces}
  - n_elements, n_spectra
  - mean_rd, median_rd, p95_rd
  - pass/fail vs threshold (5%/20%/MDL)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] tests/benchmark/test_stratified_metrics.py with fixtures that
  exercise the boundary cases (5 wt%, 0.1 wt%, exactly at LOQ)
- [ ] Synthetic test demonstrating that a "majors-only" win does NOT
  pass when trace RD increases
- [ ] Integration: comprehensive_results.json + composition_summary.json
  carry per_stratum_summary with the three tiers
- [ ] Documented in docs/VALIDATION_METRICS.md §2.1 (already specified)

title: validation-framework: robustness perturbation tests (line-dropout + outlier injection)
description:
Per docs/VALIDATION_METRICS.md §3 and protocol.yaml robustness section:
- Line-dropout: remove top-3 leverage lines, recompute d_A, require
  delta_d_A < 0.02
- Outlier injection: inject N(0, 5sigma) into 5% of channels, recompute
  d_A, require delta_d_A < 0.05

## Why

Standard CF-LIBS practice (Matsumura 2024 ACS Earth Space Chem)
demonstrates that robust likelihoods (Student-t) should beat L2 under
outlier perturbation. We currently have no perturbation harness, so
PR #58's central claim ("Student-t is more robust") is untestable.

## What to add

New cflibs/benchmark/robustness.py:

- line_dropout_perturbation(spectrum, top_n=3) -> perturbed_spectrum
- outlier_injection_perturbation(spectrum, fraction=0.05, sigma_mult=5,
  rng) -> perturbed_spectrum
- run_perturbation_battery(pipeline_fn, spectra, perturbations) ->
  PerturbationReport with per-spectrum delta_d_A

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] tests/benchmark/test_robustness.py with synthetic-spectrum
  fixtures
- [ ] Integration: scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py optionally invokes
  the perturbation battery (CLI flag --perturb)
- [ ] PR comment reports delta_d_A per perturbation type
- [ ] Tier-2 alarm if delta_d_A exceeds the protocol thresholds

## References

- Matsumura 2024 ACS Earth Space Chem
- docs/VALIDATION_METRICS.md §3
