title: Reduce noisy CLI startup warnings for beginner workflows
description: Novice-facing CLI commands currently print JAX TPU backend messages, JAX x64 warnings, and sometimes Matplotlib font/cache warnings before useful output. Suppress or defer optional imports so cflibs doctor, forward, and analyze produce cleaner beginner-facing output.

title: test_spectrum_model::test_spectrum_model_path_length_scaling fails with ratio 1.62 (expected 1.8-2.2)
description:
Pre-existing failure on main. The test asserts that doubling path_length_m approximately doubles the spectral intensity (ratio 1.8-2.2), but currently gets 1.6216. Warnings suggest the setup is borderline:

  WARNING cflibs.plasma.state:state.py:357 Charge neutrality check: n_e=1.00e+17, total_species_density=1.10e+16
  WARNING cflibs.plasma.saha_boltzmann:saha_boltzmann.py:204 No energy levels for Fe 3, using approximation
  WARNING cflibs.plasma.saha_boltzmann:saha_boltzmann.py:204 No energy levels for H 2, using approximation

The ratio deviating from 2.0 likely reflects non-linear saturation in emissivity vs. path length when optical depth becomes non-negligible at the test's chosen parameters, OR a change in the forward model between when the test was written (expected linear scaling) and the current implementation (which may include self-absorption or an optical-depth factor).

Fix options:
1. Widen the tolerance if the non-linearity is physically correct.
2. Investigate whether recent forward-model changes (e.g., self-absorption default on) changed behavior.
3. Use plasma parameters where path_length stays deep in the optically-thin regime.

Reproduction:
  .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_spectrum_model.py::test_spectrum_model_path_length_scaling -v

Assertion:
  assert 1.8 < np.float64(1.6216450040214387)

This is not a regression from swarm/3fy3 — verified by running on main.
close_reason: Fixed on swarm/3fy3 @ 374e0ca via cherry-pick from PR #46. Path-length test now uses 1e-5/2e-5 m (optically thin) instead of 0.01/0.02 m. Linear scaling property holds; ratio in [1.8, 2.2]. All 10 tests in test_spectrum_model.py pass.

title: Benchmark and tune alias.py L-BFGS-B non-negative elastic-net vs sklearn ElasticNet baseline
description:
The de-ML swap in 0ff5 replaced sklearn's Cython-optimized ElasticNet coordinate descent with scipy.optimize.minimize(L-BFGS-B). Efficiency review estimates 5-20x slowdown at default settings on typical (~50 peaks, ~50 candidates) matrices.

The function sits on the identification hot path (called once per identify_elements call, so per-spectrum, not per-evolution-candidate), but batch-identification workflows could still feel it.

Scope:
1. Benchmark _compute_sparse_nnls_scores on representative ALIAS matrices (Aalto 74 spectra via test_alias fixtures) — measure wall time before/after d33bf7b/07ad12d.
2. If regression is >2x: apply the following optimizations incrementally:
   (a) Precompute AtA = A_norm.T @ A_norm and Aty = A_norm.T @ peak_intensities once, then use quadratic form in _loss_grad (O(n^2) instead of O(mn) per call).
   (b) Drop the float(...) casts in _loss_grad — they force host sync per call.
   (c) Tune L-BFGS-B options (ftol, gtol, maxiter) to match sklearn's default early-stopping behavior rather than running to maxiter=2000.
   (d) Consider scipy.optimize.nnls on the augmented system [A_norm; sqrt(l2)*I] x = [y; 0] — closed-form, likely fastest.
3. Physics-only constraint remains absolute; no path may reintroduce ML libs. The ruff TID251 rule will catch any regression.

Not urgent — per-spectrum perf is acceptable for now. File now so the concern is tracked.

title: Update test_nist_parity.py partition function tests for direct summation
description: The current test_partition_functions compares polynomial U(T) against NIST reference values. Update to: (1) test direct summation instead of polynomials, (2) use Barklem & Collet values as reference where available, (3) widen tolerance for elements with known incomplete level data at high T, (4) add tests at ps-LIBS conditions (T=5000-15000K) where our level data is most complete.
acceptance_criteria: test_nist_parity.py passes in CI. Tests exercise direct summation path. Tolerances are physically justified.
close_reason: Tests updated: 52 passed, self-consistency + polynomial comparison tests added.

title: Validate against Barklem & Collet (2016) tabulated partition functions
description: Download Barklem & Collet (2016) partition function tables from their GitHub repo (github.com/barklem/Equilibrium). Compare our direct summation U(T) against their values for Fe I/II, Cu I/II, Al I/II, Cr I/II, Ti I/II, Ni I/II at T=3000-20000K. Document discrepancies and their causes (different energy level sets, truncation criteria). This validates our implementation against an independent authoritative source.
acceptance_criteria: Comparison table produced. Agreement within 5% for T < 15000K for all tested species, or discrepancies explained by differing energy level completeness.
close_reason: Validated against Barklem & Collet 2016. Good agreement at LIBS temps, expected divergence at high T.

title: Add JAX-compatible direct summation for manifold batch computation
description: Add jit-compiled direct_sum_partition_function_jax() that takes pre-loaded (g_array, E_array) per species and computes U(T) via jnp.sum(g * jnp.exp(-E/kT)). Must support vmap over temperature arrays. This replaces polynomial_partition_function_jax() as the default for ManifoldGenerator.
acceptance_criteria: JAX function produces identical results to NumPy version within float64 precision. Benchmarks show <10x overhead vs polynomial (acceptable for manifold generation).
close_reason: JAX jit+vmap direct summation implemented and verified.

title: Partition function polynomial fits diverge from NIST for Cr, Ti, Ni, Al II
description: test_nist_parity.py shows partition function U(T) errors >5% for Cr I (up to 66%), Ti I/II (up to 26%), Ni I/II (up to 27%), and Al II (up to 18%) compared to NIST reference values. The polynomial coefficients in libs_production.db need refitting. Root cause is likely insufficient polynomial degree or limited temperature range in the original fits from datagen_v2.py.
close_reason: Superseded by epic atp — root cause was polynomial fits, not data quality. Direct summation replaces polynomials.

title: RoundTripValidator drops elements in concentration recovery
description: RoundTripValidator.validate() recovers only a single element (Cu=1.0) and drops Fe entirely when given a Fe/Cu binary composition at T=10000K, ne=1e17 with the production DB. Temperature recovery is reasonable (5% noiseless) but concentration recovery is catastrophically wrong. Root cause is likely in the identification or closure step inside the validator — needs investigation.

title: Fix interpretable.py batch extraction needing fixed feature vocabulary
description:
## Bug: extract_batch() uses variable feature vocabulary across samples, producing misaligned matrix

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/interpretable.py`, lines 625-671

### Root Cause

The `extract_batch()` method calls `self.extract()` independently for each spectrum (line 654). Each call can produce a different set of features depending on which peaks are detected in that particular spectrum. The method captures feature names only from the first sample:

```python
for i in range(n_samples):
    unc = uncertainties[i] if uncertainties is not None else None
    result = self.extract(wavelengths, spectra[i], unc)

    if i == 0:
        all_features = result.features    # first sample's features
        feature_names = result.feature_names  # first sample's feature names

    feature_matrices.append(result.feature_matrix)  # shape (1, n_features_i)

# Stack into single matrix
feature_matrix = np.vstack(feature_matrices)  # FAILS if n_features differs
```

**Two failure modes:**

1. **np.vstack crash:** If sample 0 detects 15 features but sample 3 detects 12 features, `result.feature_matrix` has shape `(1, 15)` vs `(1, 12)`. `np.vstack()` at line 663 raises `ValueError: all the input array dimensions except for the concatenation axis must match exactly`.

2. **Silent misalignment:** If by chance all samples produce the same number of features but different features (e.g., sample 0 detects Fe/Cu ratio, sample 5 detects Fe/Ni ratio), the columns are misaligned — feature_names says "ratio_Fe_Cu" but the data in that column for sample 5 is actually "ratio_Fe_Ni".

### Impact

Batch feature extraction is fundamentally broken for heterogeneous spectra (the common case in real LIBS data, where different samples may have different elements present). It either crashes or silently produces a corrupt feature matrix.

### Fix Direction

1. First pass: extract features from all samples, collect the union of all feature names
2. Build a fixed vocabulary (sorted union of feature names)
3. Second pass (or during first pass): for each sample, map its features into the fixed vocabulary, filling missing features with 0.0
```python
all_names = set()
per_sample_features = []
for i in range(n_samples):
    result = self.extract(wavelengths, spectra[i], unc)
    per_sample_features.append({f.name: f.value for f in result.features})
    all_names.update(f.name for f in result.features)

vocab = sorted(all_names)
matrix = np.zeros((n_samples, len(vocab)))
for i, feat_dict in enumerate(per_sample_features):
    for j, name in enumerate(vocab):
        matrix[i, j] = feat_dict.get(name, 0.0)
```
acceptance_criteria: extract_batch() uses a fixed feature vocabulary across all samples; missing features filled with 0
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix line_detection.py float keys causing missed dictionary lookups
description:
## Bug: Float dictionary keys cause missed deconvolution lookups

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/identify/line_detection.py`

### Dictionary key creation (lines 438-441)

```python
deconv_results_by_wl = {}
for fr in deconv.fit_results:
    best_peak_wl = float(peak_wl_arr[np.argmin(np.abs(peak_wl_arr - fr.center_nm))])
    deconv_results_by_wl[best_peak_wl] = fr
```

### Dictionary lookup (line 481)

```python
if deconv_results_by_wl is not None and peak_wl in deconv_results_by_wl:
    result = _build_observation_from_fit(
        transition,
        deconv_results_by_wl[peak_wl],
        ground_state_threshold_ev,
    )
```

### Where `peak_wl` comes from (`_match_transitions_to_peaks`, line 906)

```python
matches.append(
    (
        transition,
        peak_idx,
        float(peak_wavelengths[best_idx]),   # <-- this becomes peak_wl
        float(deltas[best_idx]),
    )
)
```

### Problem

Two independent float conversions produce the dictionary key and lookup key:

1. **Key creation (line 440):** `best_peak_wl = float(peak_wl_arr[np.argmin(np.abs(peak_wl_arr - fr.center_nm))])` -- snaps to the nearest peak in `peak_wl_arr`, then converts to Python float.
2. **Key lookup (line 481):** `peak_wl` comes from `_match_transitions_to_peaks` (line 906): `float(peak_wavelengths[best_idx])` -- extracts the peak wavelength from the same `peaks` list, but via a different index path.

Even though both should refer to the same peak, the float values can differ because:
- `peak_wl_arr` on line 425 is built as `np.array([pw for _, pw in peaks])` while `peak_wavelengths` on line 886 is built as `np.array([p[1] for p in peaks])`. If the peaks list is reordered, filtered, or the `argmin` snaps to a different neighbor, the floats won't match.
- Even with identical source data, `float(np.float64(...))` can produce subtly different bit patterns depending on intermediate operations.

When the keys don't match exactly, `peak_wl in deconv_results_by_wl` returns `False`, silently falling through to the trapezoid integration path (`_build_observation`) instead of using the higher-quality deconvolution result.

### Fix

Replace exact float key lookup with tolerance-based matching (or use rounded keys to a fixed precision like 4 decimal places / 0.0001 nm):

**Option A (rounded keys):**
```python
ROUND_DIGITS = 4  # 0.0001 nm precision
# Key creation:
deconv_results_by_wl[round(best_peak_wl, ROUND_DIGITS)] = fr
# Lookup:
rounded_key = round(peak_wl, ROUND_DIGITS)
if deconv_results_by_wl is not None and rounded_key in deconv_results_by_wl:
    result = _build_observation_from_fit(transition, deconv_results_by_wl[rounded_key], ...)
```

**Option B (tolerance search):**
```python
def _find_deconv_result(deconv_results_by_wl, peak_wl, tol=0.001):
    for key, val in deconv_results_by_wl.items():
        if abs(key - peak_wl) < tol:
            return val
    return None
```

Option A is preferred for O(1) lookup performance.

### Context

Found by Copilot review on PR #5. The deconvolution path produces more accurate line intensities (fitted Gaussian area vs. trapezoidal sum), so missed lookups silently degrade measurement quality without any warning.
acceptance_criteria: deconv_results lookup uses tolerance-based matching or rounded keys; no missed lookups on real spectra
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix PINN log_ne not influencing forward model
description:
## Bug: PINN log_ne not influencing forward model output

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/pinn.py`

### Root Cause

The `DifferentiableForwardModel._compute_spectrum()` static method (lines 844-876) has signature:
```python
def _compute_spectrum(wavelength, T_eV, concentrations, line_positions, line_gA, line_Ek, line_element_idx, sigma_inst)
```

It does NOT accept `n_e` or `log_ne` as a parameter. The `forward()` method (lines 878-911) calls `_compute_spectrum` and passes `sigma_inst = self.instrument_fwhm / 2.355` (line 901) — a fixed constant.

**Buggy code (lines 859-876):**
```python
# Temperature-dependent Gaussian width (simplified Doppler)
sigma_doppler = 0.01 * jnp.sqrt(T_eV / 0.86)  # Approximate scaling
sigma_total = jnp.sqrt(sigma_inst**2 + sigma_doppler**2)
# ...profiles computed with sigma_total only...
```

The line width (`sigma_total`) depends only on temperature and instrument FWHM. There is no Stark broadening term, which should scale with n_e. In a real LIBS plasma, Stark broadening is the dominant broadening mechanism and scales roughly as n_e^(2/3). Without it, the forward model spectrum is invariant to n_e, meaning n_e cannot be recovered from spectral fitting.

### Impact

The neural encoder predicts `log_ne` but this parameter has zero gradient through the forward model path. Any forward-model-based loss (reconstruction loss) provides no signal for learning n_e. The network can only learn n_e from the supervised data loss term `loss_ne = (log_ne_pred - log_ne_true)**2` at line 1048, which requires labeled training data and does not generalize.

### Fix Direction

Add Stark broadening to `_compute_spectrum()`:
```python
# Stark broadening (Lorentzian HWHM ~ n_e^0.67)
n_e = 10.0 ** log_ne
stark_width = w_stark * (n_e / 1e16) ** 0.67  # per-line Stark widths
```
Either use a Voigt profile (Gaussian + Lorentzian convolution) or approximate with a pseudo-Voigt. The `log_ne` parameter must be added to the `_compute_spectrum` signature and `forward()` must pass it through.
acceptance_criteria: _compute_spectrum() uses n_e for Stark broadening; output changes when n_e varies at fixed T
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix comb.py index out-of-bounds on shifted peak lookup
description:
## Bug: Index out-of-bounds in comb.py shifted peak lookup

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/identify/comb.py`
**Lines:** 209-211

### Buggy code (lines 209-211)

```python
intensity_exp=intensity[
    np.argmin(np.abs(wavelength - tooth_result["center_nm"]))
    + tooth_result["best_shift"]
],
```

### Problem

When a tooth's center wavelength is near the start or end of the spectrum, `np.argmin(...)` returns an index near 0 or `len(intensity)-1`. Adding `best_shift` (which ranges from `-max_shift_pts` to `+max_shift_pts`, default +/-5) can push the resulting index below 0 or beyond `len(intensity)-1`, causing an `IndexError`.

For example: if `center_nm` is at the first few nm of the spectrum, `argmin` returns 0, and `best_shift = -3` yields index `-3`. NumPy will silently wrap negative indices (returning a value from the end of the array), which is semantically wrong. If the index exceeds the array length, it raises `IndexError`.

### Fix

Clamp the computed index to `[0, len(intensity) - 1]` before using it to index into `intensity`:

```python
raw_idx = (
    np.argmin(np.abs(wavelength - tooth_result["center_nm"]))
    + tooth_result["best_shift"]
)
safe_idx = max(0, min(raw_idx, len(intensity) - 1))
intensity_exp=intensity[safe_idx],
```

### Context

Found by Copilot review on PR #5, #12, #13. The `best_shift` value is determined by the `_correlate_tooth` method (line 499) which searches shifts in range `[-max_shift_pts, +max_shift_pts]`. The correlate method itself handles boundary safety (lines 504-505) but the intensity lookup in the caller does not.
acceptance_criteria: comb identifier handles peaks near spectrum boundaries without IndexError; existing tests pass
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix pca.py center=False reporting wrong variance ratios
description:
## Bug: PCA variance ratios wrong when center=False

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/common/pca.py`
**Location:** Lines 445-446, in method `fit()`

### Buggy Code (lines 437-446)
```python
# Center data
if self.center:
    mean = np.mean(X, axis=0)
    X_centered = X - mean
else:
    mean = np.zeros(n_features, dtype=np.float64)
    X_centered = X

# Total variance (before any dimensionality reduction)
total_variance = float(np.var(X_centered, axis=0, ddof=1).sum())
```

### Problem
When `center=False`, `X_centered` is just the raw data `X` (not mean-subtracted). The total variance is then computed as `np.var(X_centered, ...)` which computes the *centered* variance of the uncentered data (i.e., variance around each feature's mean).

However, the SVD of uncentered data produces singular values that capture variance around the **origin**, not around the mean. The explained variance from SVD is `S^2 / (n-1)`, which represents variance around zero. Dividing this by the centered variance gives `explained_variance_ratio` values that can exceed 1.0 or sum to more than 1.0 — which is nonsensical.

### Fix Approach
When `center=False`, compute total variance as the sum of mean squares plus variance (i.e., variance around the origin):
```python
if self.center:
    total_variance = float(np.var(X_centered, axis=0, ddof=1).sum())
else:
    # Variance around origin = E[X^2] - 0 = mean of squares (with ddof correction)
    total_variance = float(np.sum(X_centered**2) / (n_samples - 1))
```
This ensures `explained_variance_ratio` sums to <= 1.0 and is consistent with what the SVD actually decomposes.

### Impact
Any user calling `IncrementalPCA(center=False).fit(X)` or using PCA on pre-centered data gets misleading variance ratios. The `components_for_variance()` method (line 207) uses `cumulative_variance_ratio` which would also be wrong, potentially selecting too few components.
acceptance_criteria: center=False variance ratios computed from uncentered total variance; matches sklearn PCA(center=False) within 1e-6
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix PINN boltzmann/saha regularization effectively disabled during training
description:
## Bug: PINN Boltzmann/Saha regularization effectively disabled during training

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/pinn.py`

### Root Cause

`PINNInverter._loss_fn()` (line 1029-1068) calls `self.physics_layer(T_pred, log_ne_pred, conc_pred)` at line 1054 with only 3 positional args. The `PhysicsConstraintLayer.__call__()` (line 490-557) accepts two optional kwargs: `partition_funcs_neutral` and `partition_funcs_ion`, both defaulting to `None`.

Since `_loss_fn` never passes partition functions, the Saha constraint block at lines 541-557 is never entered (guarded by `if partition_funcs_neutral is not None and partition_funcs_ion is not None`). The `boltzmann_residual()` function defined at line 263 is never called anywhere in the physics layer at all — it exists as a standalone utility but the `PhysicsConstraintLayer` only computes closure, positivity, and range penalties.

**Buggy code (lines 1053-1055):**
```python
# Physics constraint losses
physics_losses = self.physics_layer(T_pred, log_ne_pred, conc_pred)
physics_loss = sum(physics_losses.values())
```

The physics_losses dict only ever contains: `closure`, `positivity`, `T_range`, `ne_range`. The `saha` key is never populated because partition functions are not passed. The `boltzmann_residual` function is never invoked.

### Impact

Training degrades to supervised data fitting with only soft constraints (sum-to-one, positivity, range bounds). The core physics of LTE — Boltzmann level populations and Saha ionization balance — are not enforced, defeating the purpose of the "physics-informed" architecture.

### Fix Direction

1. Compute or look up partition functions for each element at the predicted T during training
2. Pass them to `self.physics_layer(T_pred, log_ne_pred, conc_pred, partition_funcs_neutral=..., partition_funcs_ion=...)`
3. Add a `boltzmann_residual` call inside `PhysicsConstraintLayer.__call__()` using line energies and gA values available from the forward model
acceptance_criteria: PINNInverter._loss_fn() passes partition functions to physics layer; boltzmann_residual called during training
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix transfer.py MMD estimator including diagonal kernel terms
description:
## Bug: MMD estimator includes self-kernel diagonal terms, biasing the estimate

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/transfer.py`, lines 248-258

### Root Cause

The `compute_mmd()` function computes the unbiased MMD² estimator but includes diagonal (self-kernel) terms. The code at lines 252-256:

```python
# Unbiased estimator
mmd_squared = (
    np.sum(K_XX) / (n_X * (n_X - 1) + 1e-10)
    + np.sum(K_YY) / (n_Y * (n_Y - 1) + 1e-10)
    - 2.0 * np.sum(K_XY) / (n_X * n_Y + 1e-10)
)
```

`np.sum(K_XX)` sums ALL entries including the diagonal where `K(x_i, x_i) = 1` (for RBF kernel). The denominator `n_X * (n_X - 1)` is correct for the unbiased estimator (which should exclude diagonals), but the numerator `np.sum(K_XX)` includes them.

For the unbiased U-statistic estimator, the diagonal must be excluded:
```python
np.sum(K_XX) - np.trace(K_XX)  # or equivalently: np.sum(K_XX - np.diag(np.diag(K_XX)))
```

### Impact

For RBF kernel, each diagonal entry contributes `K(x_i, x_i) = exp(0) = 1.0`. With n samples, the diagonal adds n to the sum. This positively biases both the K_XX and K_YY terms. When X == Y (identical distributions), MMD should be ~0 but instead gives a positive bias of approximately `2 / (n - 1)`. For small sample sizes (e.g., n=10), this bias is ~0.22, which is significant enough to falsely indicate domain shift. The bias also distorts the MMD-based domain adaptation optimization in `_fit_mmd()`.

### Fix Direction

Replace `np.sum(K_XX)` with `np.sum(K_XX) - np.trace(K_XX)` and same for `K_YY`:
```python
mmd_squared = (
    (np.sum(K_XX) - np.trace(K_XX)) / (n_X * (n_X - 1) + 1e-10)
    + (np.sum(K_YY) - np.trace(K_YY)) / (n_Y * (n_Y - 1) + 1e-10)
    - 2.0 * np.sum(K_XY) / (n_X * n_Y + 1e-10)
)
```
acceptance_criteria: MMD estimator excludes self-kernel diagonal; MMD(X,X) ≈ 0 for identical distributions
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix pca.py float32 downcast in JAX backend degrading SVD accuracy
description:
## Bug: JAX PCA backend hardcodes float32, losing precision

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/common/pca.py`
**Location:** Line 618, in method `_fit_jax()`

### Buggy Code (lines 617-621)
```python
# Convert to JAX array
X_jax = jnp.array(X_centered, dtype=jnp.float32)

# JAX SVD
U, S, Vt = jnp.linalg.svd(X_jax, full_matrices=False)
```

### Problem
The JAX backend unconditionally downcasts the input data to `float32` before computing the SVD. This causes:

1. **Precision loss in singular values** — the SVD of a float32 matrix loses ~7 decimal digits of precision compared to float64, which propagates into explained variance ratios.
2. **Inconsistency with NumPy backend** — `_fit_numpy()` (line 560+) operates on the original float64 data. The same input data will produce different PCA results depending on which backend is selected, with the JAX backend being strictly worse.
3. **Ignores JAX x64 mode** — when `jax_enable_x64=True` is set (which it is in conftest.py and recommended for physics work), JAX fully supports float64. The hardcoded float32 defeats this.

### Fix Approach
Replace the hardcoded `jnp.float32` with a conditional that respects JAX's x64 configuration:
```python
dtype = jnp.float64 if jax.config.jax_enable_x64 else jnp.float32
X_jax = jnp.array(X_centered, dtype=dtype)
```
This preserves float32 performance on Metal (Apple Silicon, which doesn't support float64) while getting full precision on CPU and CUDA when x64 mode is enabled.

### Impact
Any user running PCA with `use_jax=True` on a CPU or CUDA backend with x64 enabled gets silently degraded results. For spectroscopic data with dynamic ranges spanning several orders of magnitude, the float32 truncation can cause physically meaningful differences in component loadings.
acceptance_criteria: JAX PCA backend uses float64 when jax_enable_x64=True; explained variance ratios match NumPy backend within 1e-6
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix transfer.py save/load missing PDS and standardization state
description:
## Bug: CalibrationTransfer.save()/load() missing PDS matrices and standardization state

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/transfer.py`, lines 920-968

### Root Cause

The `save()` method (lines 920-933) serializes only:
```python
data = {
    "method": self.method.name,
    "window_size": self.window_size,
    "n_components": self.n_components,
    "slope": self._slope.tolist() if self._slope is not None else None,
    "bias": self._bias.tolist() if self._bias is not None else None,
    "transfer_matrix": (
        self._transfer_matrix.tolist() if self._transfer_matrix is not None else None
    ),
}
```

**Missing state for PDS method:** The `_pds_matrices` attribute (line 664) is a list of `(start_idx, end_idx, coefficients)` tuples populated by `_fit_pds()` (lines 763-782). This is not saved. After load, `_transform_pds()` (line 852) iterates over `self._pds_matrices` which will be `None`, causing `TypeError: 'NoneType' is not iterable`.

**Missing state for STANDARDIZATION method:** `_fit_standardization()` (lines 802-811) sets `self._source_mean`, `self._source_std`, `self._target_mean`, `self._target_std`. None of these are saved. After load, `_transform_standardization()` (line 862-865) accesses these attributes, causing `AttributeError`.

The `load()` method (lines 936-968) only restores `_slope`, `_bias`, and `_transfer_matrix`, matching the incomplete save. It sets `_fitted = True` at line 967, so the loaded model passes the fit check but fails at transform time.

### Impact

Save/load is broken for 2 of the 4 implemented methods (PDS and STANDARDIZATION). Only SBC (uses slope/bias) and DS (uses transfer_matrix) round-trip correctly.

### Fix Direction

1. In `save()`, add serialization for `_pds_matrices` (list of tuples → list of dicts with start, end, coeffs.tolist()) and the four standardization arrays
2. In `load()`, restore these fields
3. Add a round-trip test: fit → save → load → transform and compare output
acceptance_criteria: save() persists PDS matrices and standardization means/stds; load() + transform() produces same output as before save
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix data_structures.py y_uncertainty returning negative for negative intensity
description:
## Bug: y_uncertainty allows division by negative intensity

**File:** `cflibs/inversion/common/data_structures.py`
**Location:** Lines 59-61, in property `y_uncertainty` of class `LineObservation`

### Buggy Code (lines 47-61)
```python
# In y_value property:
if self.intensity <= 0:
    return -np.inf

# In y_uncertainty property:
if self.intensity == 0:
    return 0.0
return self.intensity_uncertainty / self.intensity
```

### Problem
The guard conditions are inconsistent between the two sibling properties:

- `y_value` guards against `intensity <= 0` (both zero AND negative)
- `y_uncertainty` only guards against `intensity == 0` (exactly zero)

When `intensity < 0` (e.g., baseline-subtracted spectra where noise dips below zero):
- `y_value` correctly returns `-np.inf` (can't take log of negative number)
- `y_uncertainty` computes `intensity_uncertainty / negative_intensity`, returning a **negative uncertainty**

A negative uncertainty is physically meaningless and will corrupt downstream Boltzmann fitting: the weighted least-squares fit in `boltzmann.py` uses `1/uncertainty^2` as weights, and a negative uncertainty squared is still positive but the sign flip means the residual contribution is wrong (pulling the fit in the wrong direction if the uncertainty is used directly rather than squared).

### Fix Approach
Change the guard in `y_uncertainty` to match `y_value`:
```python
if self.intensity <= 0:
    return 0.0
```
This makes both properties consistent: when intensity is non-positive, `y_value` returns `-np.inf` and `y_uncertainty` returns `0.0`, signaling that this data point should be excluded from fitting.

### Impact
Any spectral line with negative intensity (common after background subtraction) produces a negative uncertainty, which can silently degrade Boltzmann plot quality. The bug is subtle because it only triggers for negative intensities, not zero.
acceptance_criteria: y_uncertainty returns 0.0 for intensity <= 0 (matching y_value guard); existing tests pass
close_reason: Closed

GO: at least one config beats hybrid_intersect by >=0.02 F1, no FP increase, composition not worse.
notes: EXECUTOR: human-only (cluster). ps-LIBS regime. Requires Phase 1 go/no-go gate passed.

title: Experiment Phase 1: Sampler feasibility (4-8 GPU-hours + 2-4 CPU-hours)
description: Run on beefcake cluster. Goal: prove Bayesian path can stably recover plasma parameters. Experiments: (a) Round-trip recovery on 48-96 synthetic spectra (pure, binary, steel-like, 4-element mixtures). (b) Compare NUTS-only (GPU) vs dynesty-only (CPU) vs NUTS+dynesty on 10-20 ambiguous spectra. (c) Test prefiltered top-K (K=4,8,12,20) speed/accuracy tradeoff. (d) Compare pre-stripped baseline vs in-model polynomial baseline. (e) Test single-zone vs two-zone model on Fe-dominated synthetics. GO criteria: >=90% runs without sampler failure, median T error <=5%, median n_e error <=20%, median composition MAE <=0.05, R-hat <1.01 and ESS >100 on >=80% of NUTS runs, PPC p-value in [0.05,0.95] for >=80%. STOP if >20% failure rate after tuning.
notes: EXECUTOR: human-only (cluster). ps-LIBS regime (1ps/1040nm): T=0.5-1.0 eV, ne=1e16-1e17. Requires all PRs merged + 1uw.6 completed first.

title: Phase 5: Documentation and final cleanup
description: Update CLAUDE.md module map to reflect new package structure. Add cflibs/pds/ documentation (ChemCam/SuperCam planetary data — currently undocumented). Update pyproject.toml extras, pytest markers, CI configs. Prune stale __pycache__ directories. Final validation: ruff check + black --check + mypy + full test suite.
notes: EXECUTOR: human/claude. Documentation accuracy requires understanding the full restructure.

title: Phase 4: Consolidate top-level directories
description: Move: arxiv/→paper/submissions/arxiv/ (remove cf-libs-gpu.tar.gz from VCS), conductor/→docs/planning/product.md, tasks/→docs/planning/prds/, legacy/→docs/archive/legacy/, cflibs-core/→native/cflibs-core/, rust-plugin/→native/rust-plugin/, validation/→tests/validation/. Update Cargo.toml workspace paths, pyproject.toml, Justfile, CI configs in one atomic commit. Validation: cargo test for Rust + pytest discovers all tests.
notes: EXECUTOR: human/claude. Atomic commit across 6 directories + Cargo.toml + pyproject.toml + Justfile.

title: Phase 3: Quarantine ML/chemometric modules
description: MUST RUN AFTER Phase 1b (both modify __init__.py). Create cflibs/experimental/ml/ and move pinn.py, interpretable.py, transfer.py from cflibs/inversion/. CORRECTIONS from rev 3 review: (1) DO NOT quarantine pca.py — it is directly imported by cflibs/manifold/vector_index.py and cflibs/manifold/basis_index.py (production code for mandatory prefilter). Move pca.py to cflibs/inversion/common/pca.py instead. (2) pls.py IS used by cflibs/benchmark/unified.py:1124 — update that import path when moving to experimental/ml/. Mark interpretable.py and transfer.py as deletion candidates. Validation: ML tests pass from new paths + import cflibs.inversion works + manifold PCA imports work + benchmark PLS workflow runs.
notes: EXECUTOR: human/claude. Nuanced pca.py exception (DO NOT quarantine), pls.py benchmark import. Swarm will miss the nuance.

title: Phase 2: Resolve benchmark/ vs benchmarks/ duplication
description: Merge cflibs/benchmarks/ (corpus.py, harness.py, metrics.py) into cflibs/benchmark/. Rename benchmarks/metrics.py to benchmark/composition_metrics.py to avoid collision. Move top-level benchmarks/ scripts to scripts/benchmarks/, figures to docs/reports/benchmarks/figures/, results to output/benchmarks/ (.gitignored). Add deprecation shim in cflibs/benchmarks/__init__.py then delete. Update all imports across codebase. Validation: all benchmark tests pass + CLI smoke for scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py.
notes: EXECUTOR: human/claude. Multi-step merge, rename collision, deprecation shim, import updates across codebase.
close_reason: PR #2 created. benchmarks/ merged into benchmark/, shims in place, all 38 tests pass.

title: Phase 1a: Extract pure data structures from boltzmann.py
description: Extract LineObservation, BoltzmannFitResult, FitMethod and other pure dataclasses from cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py into a new cflibs/inversion/common/data_structures.py module. This eliminates the cross-layer dependency that would otherwise create circular imports when splitting inversion/ into sub-packages. Update all importers to use the new path. Keep backward-compatible re-exports in boltzmann.py. Validation: python -c 'from cflibs.inversion.boltzmann import LineObservation' still works + full test suite passes.
notes: EXECUTOR: swarm-safe. Mechanical dataclass extraction, ~3 files, clear acceptance criteria.
close_reason: PR #1 created. Data structures extracted to common/data_structures.py with backward-compatible re-exports. 1038 tests pass.

title: Codebase cleanup and single-algorithm migration plan
description: Document from 3 Codex consultations: codebase reorganization (inversion split, benchmark dedup, top-level consolidation), single-algorithm recommendation (sparse Bayesian direct inversion), and experimental validation plan for beefcake cluster.
close_reason: Plan document written and pushed to feat/CF-LIBS-improved-nqs-cleanup-plan

title: Phase 4: Integration Tests
description: Create tests/test_e2e_pipeline.py with end-to-end integration tests covering the full CF-LIBS pipeline: forward model → synthetic spectrum → preprocessing → identification → Boltzmann fitting → closure → composition. Test with known ground truth at ps-LIBS conditions (T=0.8 eV, ne=4e16, Fe-Cu-Al composition). Verify round-trip recovery within tolerance.
acceptance_criteria: pytest tests/test_e2e_pipeline.py passes; temperature recovery within 10%, composition recovery within 0.05 MAE
notes: EXECUTOR: swarm-safe with review. Requires atomic database for forward model. May need to generate fixture data if DB unavailable.
close_reason: PR #20 merged — 7 self-contained E2E tests passing, production DB tracked in git, 3 pre-existing bugs fixed

title: Phase 2 & 3: Manifold + Hybrid Inference Benchmark
description: Benchmark the manifold nearest-neighbor + hybrid inference pipeline (cflibs/inversion/solve/coarse_to_fine.py) against the iterative CF-LIBS solver (cflibs/inversion/solve/iterative.py). Compare on synthetic spectra at ps-LIBS conditions. Metrics: composition accuracy (Aitchison distance), temperature/ne recovery, wall-clock time.
acceptance_criteria: Benchmark report comparing manifold+hybrid vs iterative solver on >=20 spectra with accuracy and timing metrics
notes: EXECUTOR: human/claude. Requires manifold library pre-built. Depends on benchmark harness (PR #2 merged).

title: Phase 1: Round-Trip Validation Script
description: Create scripts/benchmark_round_trip.py

title: End-to-End CF-LIBS Pipeline Implementation and Benchmarks
description: Implement and benchmark the complete CF-LIBS pipeline: round-trip validation, manifold generation, and hybrid inference.
design:
Phase 1: Implement benchmark_round_trip.py with IterativeCFLIBSSolver on a parameter grid (T: 6000-12000K, ne: 1e16-3e17 cm-3, 3 mixtures) + NoiseModel.
Phase 2: Generate Manifold using ManifoldGenerator (Fe, Cu, Si, Al, Ca, Ti), T=0.5-2eV, ne=1e16-1e18, 4096 px. Build FAISS IVFFlat/Flat index.
Phase 3: Hybrid Manifold+Gradient benchmark vs Iterative vs Manifold-only vs Gradient-only on real and synthetic spectra. Measure latency/convergence.
Phase 4: Build Integration Tests under tests/test_e2e_pipeline.py.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Round-trip validation passes targets (<5% T error, <20% ne error)
- [ ] Manifold generation succeeds for 10k+ spectra and FAISS index builds
- [ ] Hybrid Inference yields <10ms latency after JIT warmup
- [ ] Integration tests pass via pytest

title: Fix paper and slides LaTeX compile errors and warnings
description: Compile paper/ and slides/ LaTeX, fix all errors: missing TikZ positioning library, beamer nosep incompatibility, @software bib entry type not recognized. Reduce warnings.
close_reason: All LaTeX compile errors fixed: 0 errors in both paper and slides

title: Implement cross-validation pipeline (122B models)
description: After the 27B generates perturbations and they are evaluated, send the top-K solutions to both 122B models (vasp-01, vasp-02) for independent cross-validation. Each 122B reviews the code for physics correctness and identifies potential issues. Disagreements between the two 122B models are escalated to the Council. This is the reverse flow: cheap generates, smart validates.
close_reason: Obsolete — parent 4e0j is closed. Cross-validation now lives in scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py --max-outer-folds, exercised by the F1 gate (.swarm/verify.sh).

title: Implement structural mutation (Phase 4)
description: Every K batches (~10), have Opus 4.6 propose a structural mutation: a fundamentally different scoring approach, a new veto condition, a different combination formula. Uses Bayesian surprise to prioritize which structural changes to try based on which perturbation directions showed the most information gain. Produces a new code skeleton that becomes the basis for the next round of parameter perturbations.
notes: 🚨 HARDEST CONSTRAINT POINT: structural mutation is where an LLM is most likely to 'helpfully' propose 'replace the NNLS step with a small neural net' or 'learn a correction factor from the residuals'. REJECT these at the blocklist gate. See 3fy3. The council prompt must explicitly enumerate forbidden structural substitutions: no neural layers, no trained regressors, no PLS/PCR, no learned embeddings anywhere in the shipped pipeline. Only physics-structural changes allowed.
close_reason: Obsolete — parent 4e0j is closed. Structural mutation now happens via SEPL operators (python/sepl/) plus git-worktree-based PR generation.

title: Batch radiation forward model via JAX vmap
description: BUILD: Rewrite forward model as pure JAX pipeline in cflibs/radiation/batch.py + spectrum_model.py. GEMINI DEEP RESEARCH RECOMMENDATION: Systematically rewrite the NumPy/SciPy forward engine into pure jax.numpy. Emulate ExoJAX architectural patterns: @jax.jit for XLA kernel fusion, jax.vmap for batch parallelism across parameter states. Implementation: 1. Create jax_forward_model(T, n_e, C, wavelength_grid, line_db) as pure function    - All inputs as jax arrays (no Python objects, no dicts mid-computation)    - Chain: emissivity(T, n_e, C, line_db) -> broadening(profiles, T) -> convolution(spectrum, instrument) 2. Decorate with @jax.jit for compilation 3. Use jax.vmap(jax_forward_model, in_axes=(0,0,0,None,None)) for batch over plasma states    - T, n_e, C have batch dimension; wavelength_grid and line_db are shared 4. PlasmaState fields must be stacked into arrays: T_batch = jnp.array([s.T for s in states]) 5. Instrument convolution: jax.scipy.signal.fftconvolve or custom FFT-based 6. Fallback: existing ThreadPool/ProcessPool when JAX unavailable 7. Test: compare JAX batch vs sequential forward model output (rtol=1e-6) Key: bypasses Python GIL entirely. Operates at memory bandwidth limits. Expected speedup: orders of magnitude. batch_forward_model() API unchanged.
close_reason: Completed in GPD Phase 2: batch_forward.py with vmap

title: Accelerate iterative solver convergence with Anderson mixing
description: BUY: Use jaxopt.AndersonAcceleration for solver convergence in cflibs/inversion/solver.py. GEMINI DEEP RESEARCH RECOMMENDATION: Do NOT write custom Anderson acceleration. JAXopt already has a production-ready implementation. Structure the CF-LIBS inverse as a pure fixed-point function and pass to jaxopt.AndersonAcceleration. Implementation: 1. pip install jaxopt (add to pyproject.toml optional deps) 2. Refactor solve() inner loop as a pure function: g(x) -> x where x = (T, n_e, C_vector)    - Must be a pure function (no side effects) for JAXopt compatibility    - The Saha correction, Boltzmann update, and closure are composed into g(x) 3. Call jaxopt.AndersonAcceleration(g, history_size=5, ridge=1e-6).run(x0)    - history_size=5 (M=5) per Gemini recommendation    - ridge parameter (Tikhonov regularization) critical for stability 4. Fallback: existing simple iteration when JAXopt unavailable 5. Test: compare iteration count and final T,n_e,C vs simple iteration Expected: 2-5x iteration reduction, convergence time <1ms/spectrum (from 50-200ms). solve() API and convergence criteria unchanged.
close_reason: Completed in GPD Phase 2: anderson_solver.py

title: GPU FAISS for manifold nearest-neighbor inference
description: BUY: Switch to faiss-gpu with brute-force IndexFlatL2 for manifold NN in cflibs/manifold/. GEMINI DEEP RESEARCH RECOMMENDATION: Use faiss-gpu v1.10.0+ which integrates NVIDIA cuVS (CUDA Vector Search). For our 3-5 dimensional dense parameter space, use IndexFlatL2 brute-force (NOT IVFFlat approximation) — exact results with GPU parallelism. Implementation: 1. pip install faiss-gpu (requires CUDA toolkit on vasp nodes) 2. In manifold query module, detect GPU: res = faiss.StandardGpuResources(); ngpus = faiss.get_num_gpus() 3. When GPU available: gpu_index = faiss.index_cpu_to_gpu(res, 0, cpu_index) 4. Key change: replace IVFFlat with IndexFlatL2 for exact brute-force on GPU    - For 3-5D with <100M points, brute-force on GPU is faster than approximate search    - No training step needed (IndexFlatL2 is training-free) 5. Batch search: distances, indices = gpu_index.search(query_vectors, k=1) 6. Fallback: existing CPU FAISS when faiss-gpu unavailable 7. Test: compare GPU vs CPU results (must be identical for IndexFlatL2) Expected speedup: 10-50x. Microsecond-regime latency for batch queries. ManifoldPredictor API unchanged. HDF5/Zarr storage unchanged.
close_reason: Completed in GPD Phase 3: FAISS benchmark scripts

title: Resolve closure.py C_s semantics: document as number fractions
description: The closure module outputs are documented inconsistently: ClosureResult (closure.py:144) says mass/molar fraction, CFLIBSResult (solver.py:35) says mass fractions, but the solver electron-balance algebra (solver.py:470,482-489) uses C_s as heavy-particle NUMBER fractions (avg_Z = sum C_s * epsilon_s only makes sense for number fractions). Additionally, oxide mode closure fractions are incompatible with the electron-balance update. Clarify by adding explicit type annotations and docstring corrections.
close_reason: Closed

title: BUG: Partition function polynomial path ignores IPD lowered continuum
description: DESIGN CHOICE (not a bug): Polynomial partition function path (saha_boltzmann.py:179-185) uses pre-tabulated coefficients that already include natural IP truncation. When IPD lowers the continuum, the polynomial path does not adjust. This is a known approximation — polynomial coefficients cannot easily incorporate plasma-density-dependent IPD. The level-summation fallback path (lines 205-214) correctly applies IPD truncation. Consider: add a correction factor U_corrected = U_poly * (1 - correction(delta_chi)) or force level summation when IPD delta_chi > threshold.
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix emissivity.py docstring: output is W/m^3/sr not W/m^3/nm
description: calculate_line_emissivity() docstring at emissivity.py:33-36 claims units of W/m^3/nm^-1, but the function returns line-INTEGRATED emissivity (W/m^3/sr) since it does not multiply by a line profile function. The spectral emissivity (per nm) only emerges after calculate_spectrum_emissivity() applies the Gaussian/Voigt profile. Fix the docstring to match the actual output.
close_reason: Closed

title: BUG: self_absorption.py oscillator strength prefactor uses Angstrom-based constant with cm wavelength
description: LATENT BUG (currently harmless): In self_absorption.py:819-825, the oscillator strength prefactor 1.4992e-16 is the Angstrom-based value but code uses lambda_cm. The formula gives f values 1e16 too small. However, this has NO practical effect because: (1) the actual optical depth calculation (line 424-426) uses empirical scaling 1e-25, not f_ik; (2) log_gf is only used in delta_log_gf (relative comparisons where the constant offset cancels). Fix the constant to 1.4992 for correctness, but this is cosmetic in the current code.
close_reason: Closed

title: benchmark: add _alias_boltzmann_r2_sweep workflow to unified.py / workflows.py
description: Salvaged from origin/wip/2026-05-19-stash6-alias-r2-sweep. Adds ~70 LOC to cflibs/benchmark/unified.py: _alias_boltzmann_r2_sweep_configs (varies boltzmann_r2_min across {0.70, 0.80, 0.85, 0.90}) and _build_alias_boltzmann_r2_sweep_predictor (builds an ALIASIdentifier with r2_gate_mode='fixed', threading the swept value through). Registers workflow alias_boltzmann_r2_sweep in build_id_workflow_registry. Verified NOT on dev (grep returns zero for the symbol). Note: PR #178 already landed a complementary 8-cell sweep that varies the r2_gate_mode/temperature/cl flag combination — this new sweep is orthogonal (axis = the numeric R² floor, not the on/off flag set). Marginal value is limited because alias_v2 (Phase D promoted) uses r2_gate_mode='adaptive_t' which bypasses the fixed floor for cold plasma; the sweep is most informative for warm-plasma regime + the default 'fixed' gate. If still wanted, the implementation rebases cleanly onto cflibs/benchmark/workflows.py (after PR #192 merges).
acceptance_criteria: (1) cflibs/benchmark/workflows.py (post-#192) gains the two helper functions; (2) build_id_workflow_registry includes alias_boltzmann_r2_sweep; (3) tests/benchmark/test_alias_sweep_workflows_registered.py extended with assertion that the new workflow registers; (4) one end-to-end smoke test exercises the sweep on a small synthetic fixture (CPU-only, not requires_db); (5) docstring on _alias_boltzmann_r2_sweep_configs cites the sweep design + grid values.

title: tuning: relax CombIdentifier default thresholds (min_correlation, tooth_activation) — benchmark required
description: Salvaged from BOTH origin/wip/2026-05-19-stash0-pre-extraction AND origin/wip/2026-05-19-stash6-alias-r2-sweep (same change in both stashes). The stash changes cflibs/inversion/identify/comb.py defaults: min_correlation 0.12 → 0.05, tooth_activation_threshold 0.5 → 0.3. The stash also bumps relative_threshold_scale 1.5 → 2.0 and changes Tier-2 details. EVOLVE-BLOCK marker tags it as bead CF-LIBS-improved-5thd output ('Maximizes macro_recall without regressing precision > 0.02'). The values are currently 0.12/0.5 on dev. Do NOT land without benchmark evidence — the comment claims a sweep was run but the evidence is in 5thd, not in code. Acceptance requires either (a) reproducing the sweep on current Vrabel/Aalto datasets and showing macro-recall gain ≥ 0.05 with macro-precision regression ≤ 0.02, or (b) landing it as an opt-in non-default mode.
acceptance_criteria: (1) cflibs/inversion/identify/comb.py defaults updated OR new mode kwarg added; (2) Benchmark output attached to bead showing precision/recall on Vrabel + Aalto with both old and new defaults; (3) tests/test_comb_precision.py + tests/inversion/test_comb_fp_reduction.py updated to assert new defaults (or new mode kwarg behavior); (4) docs/ADR-0001 or a new ADR captures the rationale; (5) all existing pytest still green.

title: perf(voigt): sort lines by Voigt a-region before JAX vmap to reduce GPU warp divergence
description: The vmapped JAX Voigt kernel (profiles.py:569-594, called from :789-810) sees lines in source order. On a V100S, lines with very different Voigt a = gamma/(sigma*sqrt(2*pi)) end up in the same warp, increasing thread divergence. Add a host-side argsort(a) + gather before vmap. Benchmark on the production manifold-generation workload; only land if measured speedup >= 10% on V100S. Likely becomes a no-op if Zaghloul 2024 lands first (branch-free).
acceptance_criteria: (1) apply_voigt_broadening_jax sorts lines by a before vmap and reverses the sort on output; (2) V100S benchmark on the manifold hot path shows >=10% speedup vs current; (3) numerical equivalence test (sorted vs unsorted produces identical output to float32/64 rounding); (4) Grimm 2021 + Byrohl & Nelson 2025 citations.
notes: Critical files: cflibs/radiation/profiles.py:569-594 (kernel), :789-810 (apply_voigt_broadening_jax — vmap call site). Citation: Grimm et al. 2021 (HELIOS-K 2.0); Byrohl & Nelson 2025 (THOR). Sequence: do this AFTER Zaghloul 2024 Voigt migration lands; if Zaghloul is branch-free across the parameter space, this work may become unnecessary.

title: feat(bayesian): Kennedy-O'Hagan GP discrepancy delta(lambda) for non-LTE departures
description: BayesianForwardModel (forward.py:530-574) assumes strict LTE; non-LTE departures at plasma boundaries are absorbed into composition uncertainty. Add a Kennedy-O'Hagan 2001-style GP discrepancy term delta(lambda) ~ GP(0, k) to the likelihood so true physical non-LTE departures are isolated from parameter uncertainty. Use a Matern-3/2 or RBF kernel with length-scale prior tied to instrument resolution. Default off via use_discrepancy_gp flag until validated against ground-truth non-LTE spectra.
acceptance_criteria: (1) numpyro.sample('delta', GP_kernel(...)) added to forward.py likelihood; (2) GP toggleable via use_discrepancy_gp config flag (default False); (3) length-scale prior tied to instrument FWHM; (4) synthetic non-LTE test shows delta absorbs the departure while composition posterior stays close to ground truth; (5) Kennedy-O'Hagan 2001 citation in module docstring.
notes: Critical files: cflibs/inversion/solve/bayesian/forward.py:22, 530-574. Citation: Kennedy & O'Hagan 2001. Dependency: requires A_ki random-variable scaffolding from feat(bayesian): A_ki as random variable bead — both share NumPyro per-line sample sites and pytree expansion patterns.

title: perf(manifold): add GPU FAISS path with auto-detect fallback to CPU
description: cflibs/manifold/vector_index.py:295-322 supports IndexFlatL2 / IVFFlat / IVFPQ but uses CPU FAISS only. On V100S nodes, manifold nearest-neighbor lookup could move to GPU FAISS via faiss.StandardGpuResources() + index_cpu_to_gpu for sub-millisecond coarse (T, ne) estimation. Probe for GPU FAISS at runtime; transparent fallback to CPU when faiss-gpu isn't installed.
acceptance_criteria: (1) vector_index.py probes for faiss.StandardGpuResources() at runtime; (2) IndexFlatL2 transparently uses index_cpu_to_gpu when available; (3) auto-fallback when faiss-gpu missing; (4) benchmark comparing CPU vs GPU FAISS on the production manifold size; (5) optional dependency pin for faiss-gpu in pyproject.toml [cluster] extra.
notes: Critical files: cflibs/manifold/vector_index.py:295-322. Citation: standard FAISS GPU docs.

title: Stratified F1: weight rare-element recall higher (class-frequency-weighted Fbeta)
description: Currently macro_f1 = unweighted mean across elements present in any spectrum. Rare elements (Mn, Na, K) and common elements (Si, Fe, Ca) get equal weight, which can mask rare-element failure modes. Acceptance: add macro_f1_class_weighted to the per-identifier aggregates, computed as (1/n_spectra_with_element)-weighted mean. Multi-metric gate accepts this as additional success metric.

title: Add 3-fold CV to scripts/run_unified_benchmark.py --cv-folds — measure F1 stability
description: Currently --max-outer-folds 1 means a single train/test split. Adding optional CV reveals whether F1 numbers are stable across folds or fold-dependent. Acceptance: --cv-folds 3 produces composition_summary.json with mean_aitchison ± stdev across folds. Multi-metric gate uses the mean.

Write everything to a single Parquet file per experiment (or per cell) with a schema that includes run metadata (cell, identifier, platform, seed, ...). Completed in PR #126 (merged ba1f043 to dev).

Replaced per-iter JSON record dumps with single results.parquet per
experiment. Key deliverables:

* cflibs/benchmark/results.py — new write_parquet / read_parquet
  module with unified row schema, run-metadata columns, append +

  --experiment-label, --cell, --seed, --iter-index, --platform.

title: T2.4: Pre-built basis libraries shared via NFS
description: Basis-driven identifiers (spectral_nnls, nnls_concentration_threshold) need output/basis_libraries/ which currently doesn't exist on vasp-01/02. Build the basis libraries once via scripts/build_basis_library.py (or equivalent), commit to NFS at /cluster/shared/cf-libs-bench/basis_libraries, default --basis-dir to point there. Acceptance: spectral_nnls identifier successfully runs on all 3 nodes without per-node basis rebuild.
notes: First build crashed at 04:20 on first .h5 write with PermissionError — slurm-ctl had drwxr-sr-x root:hpc and NFS root_squash maps vasp-02 root to nobody. Fixed with chmod 1777 /cluster/shared/cf-libs-bench/basis_libraries on slurm-ctl (sticky+world-writable). Write test confirmed. Restarted at 10:42Z (PID 158661 on vasp-02), grinding from FWHM 0.05 element 1/83. Will close once 8 .h5 files + manifest.json land on NFS.
close_reason: Code (PR #127, squash 3063a0c) + artifact both live. /cluster/shared/cf-libs-bench/basis_libraries/ now contains 8 FWHM .h5 files (51-204 MB, 995 MB total) + manifest.json. md5sum ed9e3a9a42296bacf8852475d5744b89 verified identical across vasp-01/02/03. Build wall: ~80 min on vasp-02 CPU. First attempt crashed on NFS write permission (root_squash + drwxr-sr-x); fixed with chmod 1777 on slurm-ctl, restarted, completed successfully. Acceptance met: spectral_nnls / hybrid_intersect / hybrid_union / nnls_concentration_threshold identifiers now have basis files on all 3 vasp nodes without per-node rebuild.

title: T2.3: Multi-armed bandit iteration allocator (Thompson sampling)
description: After a warmup phase (N=2 iters per cell), use Thompson sampling or UCB to re-allocate remaining iterations to the cells with the most promising d_A trajectories. Same total compute, far more bootstrap precision on top performers, fewer wasted iters on clearly-worse configs. Acceptance: in a fixed compute budget, bandit-allocated runs produce tighter CI on the best cell than equal-allocation does.
close_reason: PR #131 merged-pending: cflibs.bandit.ThompsonAllocator + parameter_sweep --bandit/--cells. 94.4% to good arms / 41.6% CI tighter on synthetic 6-arm x 48-iter x 20-seed. 22 tests in tests/bandit/. --bandit 0 byte-identical to T1.1.

title: T2.1: Long-lived cflibs.parameter_sweep_server worker daemon
description: Build a daemon that runs on each node, accepts config requests over a Unix socket / TCP, executes one analysis per request using a shared (preloaded) dataset + JAX JIT cache + atomic DB connection. Iter overhead becomes just analysis time + RPC round-trip. Cold start once per *experiment*, not per *iter*. Acceptance: a sequence of 8 config requests over the socket completes in <1/4 the wall time of 8 separate run_unified_benchmark.py invocations.
close_reason: Daemon shipped via PR #128 (squash 94c9cfb83a). Files: cflibs/parameter_sweep_server/{__init__,protocol,server,client}.py + scripts/start_sweep_server.sh + tests/parameter_sweep_server/{test_protocol,test_e2e}.py + docs/parameter-sweep-server-consultation.md. 16/16 fast tests pass; slow microbench validates the bd <1/4 wall-time gate on real data when ASD_da+data/ present. Live benchmark on bhvo2_usgs: boot 2.6s, REQ1 21s cold-JIT, REQ2/3 ~11s warm-JIT, 1.55x speedup at n=3. Cross-model consult (Codex + Opus 4.7) captured at docs/parameter-sweep-server-consultation.md.

title: validation-framework: adversarial-twin benchmark run
description:
Per docs/VALIDATION_METRICS.md §6, an adversarial-twin parallel agent
is told the metric AND told to game it. If the twin's gains match a
candidate PR's, the candidate is presumed gamed and rejected.

## Why

The strongest Goodhart hedge: explicitly construct an adversary that
WILL find loopholes. If a candidate's improvement is reproducible by
a metric-aware adversary, it's almost certainly gaming, not science.
Anthropic / OpenAI 2024-2025 reward-hacking literature documents this
as the only effective defense against capable agents.

## Scope (large; deferred)

Requires: a separate orchestrator instance with a "game-the-metric"
profile, prompt-engineering work, and infrastructure for parallel
benchmark dispatch. Scope ~1 week of work.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] An adversarial profile in beefcake-swarm config/profiles.yaml
- [ ] An adversarial benchmark runner that reports per-metric gains
- [ ] PR-gate logic: if adversarial twin's gains overlap candidate's
  by > 50%, block merge
- [ ] Documented edge cases (what if both fail? what if adversarial
  also produces a real improvement?)
notes: swarm opened PR: https://github.com/TheFermiSea/CF-LIBS-improved/pull/88

title: Federation sync timestamp cycle in bd shared-server mode
description: bd federation sync from a shared-server-mode workspace (e.g. beefcake-swarm via ai-proxy) repeatedly fails with 'local changes would be stomped by merge: federation_peers' because each sync attempt updates last_sync on the shared Dolt server's working set, and the laptop's bd auto-commit setting doesn't propagate through to the remote dolt session. Workaround: use 'bd dolt push --remote <name>' / 'bd dolt pull --remote <name>' directly (push works, sync's merge step does not). Reproduced on beads 1.0.3 / Dolt 1.87.0. Per-project mode (CF-LIBS-improved style) is unaffected because bd controls the local dolt session directly.
